texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Grading the evidence
texts.text: Grading the evidence

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The evidence for these recommendaTons has been assessed using the modified GRADE system. (5) The modified GRADE system defines both the strength of the recommendaTons of the guideline authors and the level of evidence upon which each of the recommendaTons is based. This grading system classifies expert recommendaTons as 'strong' (Grade 1) or 'weak' (Grade 2) based upon the balance between the benefits and risks, burden and cost. The quality or level of evidence is designated as high (Grade A), moderate (Grade B), low (Grade C) or very low (D) depending on factors such as study design, directness of evidence and consistency of results. Grades of recommendaTon and quality of evidence may range from 1A to 2D.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Executive Summary
texts.text: Executive Summary

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Anaemia is common in chronic kidney disease (CKD) encompassing non-dialysis dependent CKD (NDD-CKD) and dialysis dependent CKD (DD-CKD); people on peritoneal dialysis (PD) and haemodialysis (HD); and kidney transplant recipients (KTR).  Iron deficiency and erythropoieTn deficiency (a consequence of impaired oxygen sensing by the failing kidneys) are perhaps the most common causes of anaemia in people with CKD, especially those requiring kidney replacement therapy (KRT/dialysis).  The Renal NaTonal Service Framework and NaTonal InsTtute for Health and Clinical Excellence in the UK, and Kidney Disease Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO), all advocate treatment of anaemia in people with CKD.  Blood transfusions are infrequently required, and newer therapies such as Hypoxia-Inducible Factor (HIF-PHI) stabilisers are now in current use. Anaemia is a major clinical problem in people suffering from CKD. This guideline adds to the previous 2017 and updated 2020 anaemia guidelines. Specifically, we aim to:
texts.text: Anaemia is common in chronic kidney disease (CKD) encompassing non-dialysis dependent CKD (NDD-CKD) and dialysis dependent CKD (DD-CKD); people on peritoneal dialysis (PD) and haemodialysis (HD); and kidney transplant recipients (KTR).  Iron deficiency and erythropoieTn deficiency (a consequence of impaired oxygen sensing by the failing kidneys) are perhaps the most common causes of anaemia in people with CKD, especially those requiring kidney replacement therapy (KRT/dialysis).  The Renal NaTonal Service Framework and NaTonal InsTtute for Health and Clinical Excellence in the UK, and Kidney Disease Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO), all advocate treatment of anaemia in people with CKD.  Blood transfusions are infrequently required, and newer therapies such as Hypoxia-Inducible Factor (HIF-PHI) stabilisers are now in current use. Anaemia is a major clinical problem in people suffering from CKD. This guideline adds to the previous 2017 and updated 2020 anaemia guidelines. Specifically, we aim to:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Provide guidance on the use of iron in people with NDD-CKD and DD-CKD with added informaTon from several RCTs.
texts.text: Provide guidance on the use of iron in people with NDD-CKD and DD-CKD with added informaTon from several RCTs.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Comment on iron deficiency without anaemia in people with CKD.
texts.text: Comment on iron deficiency without anaemia in people with CKD.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Provide further informaTon on anaemia management in people with a transplant.
texts.text: Provide further informaTon on anaemia management in people with a transplant.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. Provide guidance in the use of the new HIF-PHI drugs in people with NDD-CKD and comment on use in DD-CKD.
texts.text: Provide guidance in the use of the new HIF-PHI drugs in people with NDD-CKD and comment on use in DD-CKD.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. Comment on the potenTal new therapies to be aware of for future.
texts.text: Comment on the potenTal new therapies to be aware of for future.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6. Support safe implementaTon of therapies.
texts.text: Support safe implementaTon of therapies.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 7. Update audit and research recommendaTons.
texts.text: Update audit and research recommendaTons.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We offer evidence based graded pracTce guidance covering appropriate use of HIF-PHI in people with CKD, accompanied by recommendaTons for implementaTon, clinical research and audits.
texts.text: We offer evidence based graded pracTce guidance covering appropriate use of HIF-PHI in people with CKD, accompanied by recommendaTons for implementaTon, clinical research and audits.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Introduction
texts.text: Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This 2024 clinical pracTce guideline provides updated recommendaTons on the management of Anaemia of Chronic Kidney Disease (ACKD) including people receiving kidney replacement therapy and serves to supersede all previously published UK guidelines. RecommendaTons have been graded using the modified GRADE system to indicate both the strength of each recommendaTon (strong or weak) and level of evidence for the recommendaTon (A-D) (1, 2) . The UK Kidney AssociaTon (UKKA) endorses the NICE Guideline for anaemia management in Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) 2021 (3) .
texts.text: This 2024 clinical pracTce guideline provides updated recommendaTons on the management of Anaemia of Chronic Kidney Disease (ACKD) including people receiving kidney replacement therapy and serves to supersede all previously published UK guidelines. RecommendaTons have been graded using the modified GRADE system to indicate both the strength of each recommendaTon (strong or weak) and level of evidence for the recommendaTon (A-D) (1, 2) . The UK Kidney AssociaTon (UKKA) endorses the NICE Guideline for anaemia management in Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) 2021 (3) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This 2024 guideline update covers the management of anaemia in adults, children and young people with anaemia associated with CKD. While there is no universally accepted classificaTon for categorising the populaTon with anaemia of CKD by age, this guideline adopts the classificaTon set out in NICE Guideline (4) defined as follows:
texts.text: This 2024 guideline update covers the management of anaemia in adults, children and young people with anaemia associated with CKD. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Background
texts.text: Background

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Anaemia is a common complicaTon of CKD. It is associated with leV ventricular dysfuncTon and heart failure, in addiTon to a reducTon in exercise capacity and quality of life.
texts.text: Anaemia is a common complicaTon of CKD. It is associated with leV ventricular dysfuncTon and heart failure, in addiTon to a reducTon in exercise capacity and quality of life.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The recent 25 th UK Renal Registry report (2021 data) shows that 53.8% of people receiving KRT had a Hb <100 g/L. The median haemoglobin (Hb) for kidney transplant recipients, people on peritoneal dialysis (PD) and haemodialysis was 107, 106 and 94 g/L respecTvely (9).
texts.text: The recent 25 th UK Renal Registry report (2021 data) shows that 53.8% of people receiving KRT had a Hb <100 g/L. The median haemoglobin (Hb) for kidney transplant recipients, people on peritoneal dialysis (PD) and haemodialysis was 107, 106 and 94 g/L respecTvely (9).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Indeed, over 20% of people with DD-CKD have a Hb <90 g/L. In the UK, including Scotland, 20.3% of people receiving in-centre HD had a Hb <100 g/L and 20.9% had a Hb >120 g/L; 13.3% had a ferritin <200 µg/L. The 25 th UK Renal Registry report (2021 data) also shows that 22.1% of people receiving PD in the UK had a Hb <100 g/L and 24.2% had a Hb >120 g/L. 18.8% had a ferriTn < 100 µg/L.
texts.text: Indeed, over 20% of people with DD-CKD have a Hb <90 g/L. In the UK, including Scotland, 20.3% of people receiving in-centre HD had a Hb <100 g/L and 20.9% had a Hb >120 g/L; 13.3% had a ferritin <200 µg/L. The 25 th UK Renal Registry report (2021 data) also shows that 22.1% of people receiving PD in the UK had a Hb <100 g/L and 24.2% had a Hb >120 g/L. 18.8% had a ferriTn < 100 µg/L.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Iron therapies, Erythropoiesis STmulaTng Agents (ESAs) and more recently Hypoxia Inducible Factor inhibitors (HIF-PHI) have allowed improved anaemia management in people with CKD (including those on kidney replacement therapy).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Assessment for Anaemia of CKD (Guidelines 1.1 - 1.5)
texts.text: 1. Assessment for Anaemia of CKD (Guidelines 1.1 - 1.5)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 1.1 - Evalua9on of Chronic Anaemia - Screening for Anaemia:
texts.text: Guideline 1.1 - Evalua9on of Chronic Anaemia - Screening for Anaemia:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that haemoglobin (Hb) levels should be rouTnely measured to screen for anaemia:
texts.text: We suggest that haemoglobin (Hb) levels should be rouTnely measured to screen for anaemia:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · At least annually in people with CKD G3
texts.text: At least annually in people with CKD G3

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · At least twice a year in people with CKD G4-5 not on kidney replacement therapy (2B)
texts.text: At least twice a year in people with CKD G4-5 not on kidney replacement therapy (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 1.2 - Evalua9on of Anaemia - Haemoglobin Level:
texts.text: Guideline 1.2 - Evalua9on of Anaemia - Haemoglobin Level:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that all people with anaemia associated with chronic kidney disease should be invesTgated for the cause and possible treatment, irrespecTve of the grade of kidney disease or requirement for kidney replacement therapy if:
texts.text: We recommend that all people with anaemia associated with chronic kidney disease should be invesTgated for the cause and possible treatment, irrespecTve of the grade of kidney disease or requirement for kidney replacement therapy if:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Their haemoglobin (Hb) levels are less than 110 g/L (less than 105 g/L if younger than 2 years), or they develop symptoms aXributable to anaemia. This is to ensure the correct diagnosis and management of anaemia. (1A)
texts.text: Their haemoglobin (Hb) levels are less than 110 g/L (less than 105 g/L if younger than 2 years), or they develop symptoms aXributable to anaemia. This is to ensure the correct diagnosis and management of anaemia. (1A)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 1.3 - Evalua9on of Anaemia - Kidney Func9on:
texts.text: Guideline 1.3 - Evalua9on of Anaemia - Kidney Func9on:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that CKD should be considered as a possible cause of anaemia when the glomerular filtraTon rate (GFR) is < 60 ml/min/1.73m 2 . It is more likely to be the cause if the GFR is <30ml/min/1.73m 2 (< 45 ml/min/ 1.73m 2 in people with diabetes) and no other cause, e.g., blood loss, folic acid or vitamin B12 deficiency, is idenTfied . (2B)
texts.text: We suggest that CKD should be considered as a possible cause of anaemia when the glomerular filtraTon rate (GFR) is < 60 ml/min/1.73m 2 . It is more likely to be the cause if the GFR is <30ml/min/1.73m 2 (< 45 ml/min/ 1.73m 2 in people with diabetes) and no other cause, e.g., blood loss, folic acid or vitamin B12 deficiency, is idenTfied . (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 1.4 - Evalua9on of Anaemia - Erythropoie9n Measurement:
texts.text: Guideline 1.4 - Evalua9on of Anaemia - Erythropoie9n Measurement:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that measurement of erythropoieTn levels should NOT rouTnely be considered for the diagnosis or management of anaemia for people with CKD. (1A)
texts.text: We recommend that measurement of erythropoieTn levels should NOT rouTnely be considered for the diagnosis or management of anaemia for people with CKD. (1A)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 1.5 - Evalua9on of Anaemia - Baseline Inves9ga9ons:
texts.text: Guideline 1.5 - Evalua9on of Anaemia - Baseline Inves9ga9ons:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that iniTal clinical and laboratory evaluaTon of anaemia should be performed prior to iniTaTon of treatment for anaemia in people with CKD. (1A)
texts.text: We recommend that iniTal clinical and laboratory evaluaTon of anaemia should be performed prior to iniTaTon of treatment for anaemia in people with CKD. (1A)

texts.orig: Guideline 1.5.1
texts.text: Guideline 1.5.1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that laboratory evaluaTon should include the following tests (1B):
texts.text: We recommend that laboratory evaluaTon should include the following tests (1B):

texts.label: list_item

texts.text: Full blood count (FBC) in addiTon to the Hb concentraTon

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Red blood cell indices:
texts.text: Red blood cell indices:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Mean corpuscular haemoglobin [MCH]
texts.text: Mean corpuscular haemoglobin [MCH]

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Mean corpuscular volume [MCV]
texts.text: Mean corpuscular volume [MCV]

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Mean corpuscular haemoglobin concentraTon [MCHC])
texts.text: Mean corpuscular haemoglobin concentraTon [MCHC])

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · White blood cell count and differenTal count
texts.text: White blood cell count and differenTal count

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Platelet count
texts.text: Platelet count

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Absolute reTculocyte count to assess bone marrow responsiveness (if indicated)
texts.text: Absolute reTculocyte count to assess bone marrow responsiveness (if indicated)

texts.text: Tests to Determine Iron Status:

texts.orig: · Percentage of hypochromic red blood cells (% HRC), but only if processing of blood sample is possible within 6 hours or
texts.text: Percentage of hypochromic red blood cells (% HRC), but only if processing of blood sample is possible within 6 hours or

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · ReTculocyte Hb count (CHr) or equivalent tests e.g., reTculocyte Hb equivalent (RET-He) or

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Treatment of Anaemia of CKD with Iron (Guidelines 2.1 - 2.7)
texts.text: 2. Treatment of Anaemia of CKD with Iron (Guidelines 2.1 - 2.7)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.1 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron therapy - Iron Reple9on:
texts.text: Guideline 2.1 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron therapy - Iron Reple9on:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that people should be iron replete to achieve and aim to maintain target Hb range, whether receiving ESA or HIF-PHI or not. (1B)
texts.text: We recommend that people should be iron replete to achieve and aim to maintain target Hb range, whether receiving ESA or HIF-PHI or not. (1B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Iron repleTon is usually defined as:
texts.text: Iron repleTon is usually defined as:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · %HRC <6% / CHr or RET-He >31 pg / ferriTn and TSAT (>100 microgram/L and >20%) in people with non-dialysis dependent CKD (NDD-CKD) or on PD and >200 microgram/L in people on HD. (2B)
texts.text: %HRC <6% / CHr or RET-He >31 pg / ferriTn and TSAT (>100 microgram/L and >20%) in people with non-dialysis dependent CKD (NDD-CKD) or on PD and >200 microgram/L in people on HD. (2B)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · A target ferriTn level greater than 100 microgram/L for children with CKD on dialysis as well as children with CKD not on ESA or HIF-PHI therapy. (not graded)
texts.text: A target ferriTn level greater than 100 microgram/L for children with CKD on dialysis as well as children with CKD not on ESA or HIF-PHI therapy. (not graded)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · In ensuring iron repleTon, iron should be considered when ferriTn is <500 mcg/L and/or the TSAT <30%.
texts.text: In ensuring iron repleTon, iron should be considered when ferriTn is <500 mcg/L and/or the TSAT <30%.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.2 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron Therapy - Ini9a9on of ESA/HIF-PHI and Iron Status:
texts.text: Guideline 2.2 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron Therapy - Ini9a9on of ESA/HIF-PHI and Iron Status:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that people on ESA or HIF-PHI therapy should remain iron replete before and during therapy either with oral iron or intravenous iron. (1B)
texts.text: We recommend that people on ESA or HIF-PHI therapy should remain iron replete before and during therapy either with oral iron or intravenous iron. (1B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.2.1
texts.text: Guideline 2.2.1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that ESA or HIF-PHI therapy should not be iniTated in the presence of absolute iron deficiency (ferriTn <100 mcg/L in NDD-CKD and <200 mcg/L in DD-CKD) or funcTonal Iron deficiency (TSAT <20% will normal or elevated ferriTn levels) unTl this is corrected and anaemia persists. Iron supplements should be given prior to or when iniTaTng ESA or HIF-PHI therapy. (1B)
texts.text: We recommend that ESA or HIF-PHI therapy should not be iniTated in the presence of absolute iron deficiency (ferriTn <100 mcg/L in NDD-CKD and <200 mcg/L in DD-CKD) or funcTonal Iron deficiency (TSAT <20% will normal or elevated ferriTn levels) unTl this is corrected and anaemia persists. Iron supplements should be given prior to or when iniTaTng ESA or HIF-PHI therapy. (1B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.2.2
texts.text: Guideline 2.2.2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that to define funcTonal iron deficiency (FID) ('iron restricted erythropoiesis'), a TSAT <20% and a normal or elevated ferriTn in people with NDD-CKD or maintained on PD and in those receiving HD be used. Normal or high serum ferriTn values do not exclude iron deficiency, as it could be due to other causes such as infecTon or inflammaTon. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest that to define funcTonal iron deficiency (FID) ('iron restricted erythropoiesis'), a TSAT <20% and a normal or elevated ferriTn in people with NDD-CKD or maintained on PD and in those receiving HD be used. Normal or high serum ferriTn values do not exclude iron deficiency, as it could be due to other causes such as infecTon or inflammaTon. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.3 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron Therapy - Route of Administra9on:
texts.text: Guideline 2.3 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron Therapy - Route of Administra9on:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that for people with CKD not requiring HD, or people currently receiving PD who are being considered for ESA therapy, intravenous iron should be considered to reduce ESA dose requirements. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest that for people with CKD not requiring HD, or people currently receiving PD who are being considered for ESA therapy, intravenous iron should be considered to reduce ESA dose requirements. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.3.1
texts.text: Guideline 2.3.1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest for people with NDD-CKD or maintained on PD (i.e. not requiring HD), the choice between oral vs. parenteral iron depends on a shared decision and should include the impact of the
texts.text: We suggest for people with NDD-CKD or maintained on PD (i.e. not requiring HD), the choice between oral vs. parenteral iron depends on a shared decision and should include the impact of the

texts.text: UK Kidney Associa.on Clinical Prac.ce Guidelines for Anaemia of Chronic Kidney Disease                                                                             9

texts.label: text
texts.orig: severity of iron deficiency, the previous response and side effects, the availability of venous access and the need to iniTate ESA or HIF-PHI therapy. (2A)
texts.text: severity of iron deficiency, the previous response and side effects, the availability of venous access and the need to iniTate ESA or HIF-PHI therapy. (2A)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.3.2
texts.text: Guideline 2.3.2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that most people receiving haemodialysis will require IV iron. (1A)
texts.text: We recommend that most people receiving haemodialysis will require IV iron. (1A)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.3.3
texts.text: Guideline 2.3.3

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest when offering IV iron therapy to people not receiving in-centre HD, consider high dose, low frequency (HiD/LF) IV iron as the treatment of choice for adults and young people when trying to achieve iron repleTon, considering all of the following (2B):
texts.text: We suggest when offering IV iron therapy to people not receiving in-centre HD, consider high dose, low frequency (HiD/LF) IV iron as the treatment of choice for adults and young people when trying to achieve iron repleTon, considering all of the following (2B):

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Availability of venous access
texts.text: Availability of venous access

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Preferences of the person with anaemia of CKD or, where appropriate, their family or carers
texts.text: Preferences of the person with anaemia of CKD or, where appropriate, their family or carers

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Nursing and administraTon costs
texts.text: Nursing and administraTon costs

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Cost of local drug supply
texts.text: Cost of local drug supply

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Provision of resuscitaTon faciliTes
texts.text: Provision of resuscitaTon faciliTes

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.3.4
texts.text: Guideline 2.3.4

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest the frequency of administraTon of HiD/LF IV iron for people with CKD not requiring HD and for people receiving PD needs to be tailored for each person as there are no clear data. (not graded)
texts.text: We suggest the frequency of administraTon of HiD/LF IV iron for people with CKD not requiring HD and for people receiving PD needs to be tailored for each person as there are no clear data. (not graded)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.4 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron Therapy - Upper Limit for Iron Therapy:
texts.text: Guideline 2.4 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron Therapy - Upper Limit for Iron Therapy:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For people not on HD, we recommend that serum ferriTn should not exceed 600 mcg/L in those treated with iron. To achieve this, iron management should be reviewed when ferriTn is >500 mcg/L, recognising that a level of >800 mcg/L may reflect iron toxicity. (1B)
texts.text: For people not on HD, we recommend that serum ferriTn should not exceed 600 mcg/L in those treated with iron. To achieve this, iron management should be reviewed when ferriTn is >500 mcg/L, recognising that a level of >800 mcg/L may reflect iron toxicity. (1B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.4.1
texts.text: Guideline 2.4.1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For people receiving HD, we recommend that proacTve high-dose IV iron, iniTally 600 mg in divided doses in the first month and then 400 mg every month (or equivalent), should be given unless ferriTn >700 mcg/L or TSAT >40%. (1A)
texts.text: For people receiving HD, we recommend that proacTve high-dose IV iron, iniTally 600 mg in divided doses in the first month and then 400 mg every month (or equivalent), should be given unless ferriTn >700 mcg/L or TSAT >40%. (1A)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.4.2
texts.text: Guideline 2.4.2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest a tailored approach by clinicians to assess the risk and benefit of starTng a proacTve IV iron protocol in people who have been established on HD for >12 months. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest a tailored approach by clinicians to assess the risk and benefit of starTng a proacTve IV iron protocol in people who have been established on HD for >12 months. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.5 - Treatment of Iron Deficiency without Anaemia:
texts.text: Guideline 2.5 - Treatment of Iron Deficiency without Anaemia:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend for people with CKD with iron deficiency without anaemia and concomitant heart failure, administraTon of IV iron to improve well-being, physical funcTon and to reduce heart failure admissions. (1A)
texts.text: We recommend for people with CKD with iron deficiency without anaemia and concomitant heart failure, administraTon of IV iron to improve well-being, physical funcTon and to reduce heart failure admissions. (1A)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.5.1
texts.text: Guideline 2.5.1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest for people with CKD with iron deficiency but without anaemia and no concomitant heart failure, a trial of oral or IV iron for improvement in clinical symptoms such as restless legs. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest for people with CKD with iron deficiency but without anaemia and no concomitant heart failure, a trial of oral or IV iron for improvement in clinical symptoms such as restless legs. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.6 - Administra9on of IV Iron - Safety Recommenda9ons:
texts.text: Guideline 2.6 - Administra9on of IV Iron - Safety Recommenda9ons:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that resuscitaTve medicaTon and personnel trained to evaluate and resuscitate be present in the event of anaphylaxis at each administraTon of intravenous iron. (1A)
texts.text: We recommend that resuscitaTve medicaTon and personnel trained to evaluate and resuscitate be present in the event of anaphylaxis at each administraTon of intravenous iron. (1A)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.6.1
texts.text: Guideline 2.6.1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend avoiding parenteral iron therapy in people with acTve infecTon. (1C)
texts.text: We recommend avoiding parenteral iron therapy in people with acTve infecTon. (1C)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Guideline 2.6.2 We suggest avoiding iron therapy in people with the following condiTons (not graded):
texts.text: Guideline 2.6.2 We suggest avoiding iron therapy in people with the following condiTons (not graded):

texts.text: HepaTTs C virus infecTon (PosiTve PCR test)

texts.text: Heterozygous for any of the haemochromatosis genes (C282Y (c.845G>A), H63D (c.187C>G) and S65C (c.193A>T).

texts.label: section_header

texts.text: Guideline 2.6.4

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest choice of parenteral iron be tailored, considering the benefits and risks associated with available iron preparaTons. (2C)
texts.text: We suggest choice of parenteral iron be tailored, considering the benefits and risks associated with available iron preparaTons. (2C)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.7 - Monitoring of Treatment - Iron Therapy:
texts.text: Guideline 2.7 - Monitoring of Treatment - Iron Therapy:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend regular monitoring of iron status (every 1-3 months) in people receiving intravenous iron to avoid toxicity (defined as a serum ferriTn of > 800 mcg/L or TSAT >40%). (1B)
texts.text: We recommend regular monitoring of iron status (every 1-3 months) in people receiving intravenous iron to avoid toxicity (defined as a serum ferriTn of > 800 mcg/L or TSAT >40%). (1B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.7.1
texts.text: Guideline 2.7.1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that a serum ferriTn consistently >800 mcg/L with no evidence of inflammaTon (normal CRP) is suggesTve of iron overload or potenTal toxicty. (1B)
texts.text: We recommend that a serum ferriTn consistently >800 mcg/L with no evidence of inflammaTon (normal CRP) is suggesTve of iron overload or potenTal toxicty. (1B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Treatment of Anaemia with Erythropoiesis Stimulating Agents (Guidelines 3.13.17)
texts.text: 3. Treatment of Anaemia with Erythropoiesis Stimulating Agents (Guidelines 3.13.17)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 3.1- Inves9ga9ons Before Ini9a9ng ESA Therapy:
texts.text: Guideline 3.1- Inves9ga9ons Before Ini9a9ng ESA Therapy:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that all correctable causes of anaemia should be ruled out before considering treatment with ESAs . (1B)
texts.text: We recommend that all correctable causes of anaemia should be ruled out before considering treatment with ESAs . (1B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 3.2 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron Therapy - Ini9a9on of ESA and Iron Status:
texts.text: Guideline 3.2 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron Therapy - Ini9a9on of ESA and Iron Status:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that ESA therapy should NOT be iniTated in the presence of absolute iron deficiency, (ferriTn <100 mcg/L in people with non-dialysis dependent CKD (NDD-CKD) and <200 mcg/L in people who are dialysis dependent) unTl this is corrected and it is determined that anaemia persists, in conjuncTon with a shared decision of the advantages and risks of ESA therapy. In people with funcTonal iron deficiency, iron supplements should be given prior to or when iniTaTng ESA therapy. (1B)
texts.text: We recommend that ESA therapy should NOT be iniTated in the presence of absolute iron deficiency, (ferriTn <100 mcg/L in people with non-dialysis dependent CKD (NDD-CKD) and <200 mcg/L in people who are dialysis dependent) unTl this is corrected and it is determined that anaemia persists, in conjuncTon with a shared decision of the advantages and risks of ESA therapy. In people with funcTonal iron deficiency, iron supplements should be given prior to or when iniTaTng ESA therapy. (1B)

(1B)

texts.orig: We recommend that people with non-dialysis dependent CKD (NDD-CKD) or those receiving dialysis, who are on ESA therapy, should achieve Hb between:

texts.text: Guideline 3.7 - Treatment of Anaemia - Ini9al ESA Dose:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that the iniTal ESA dose should be determined by the individual's Hb level, the desired target Hb range, the observed rate of increase in Hb level and clinical circumstances. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest that the iniTal ESA dose should be determined by the individual's Hb level, the desired target Hb range, the observed rate of increase in Hb level and clinical circumstances. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 3.8 - Treatment of Anaemia with ESA Therapy - Route of Administra9on:
texts.text: Guideline 3.8 - Treatment of Anaemia with ESA Therapy - Route of Administra9on:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that the route of ESA administraTon should be determined by the CKD grade, individual recipient preference, treatment seLng, efficacy, safety and class of ESA used. Subcutaneous (SC) use is preferable in people who are not receiving HD to avoid puncture of peripheral veins. Subcutaneous administraTon of short-acTng ESA preparaTons may be preferred in people receiving haemodialysis due to improved ESA efficacy at the EPO receptor, allowing for smaller doses to be used. However, convenience and choice may favour intravenous (IV) administraTon in people on HD. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest that the route of ESA administraTon should be determined by the CKD grade, individual recipient preference, treatment seLng, efficacy, safety and class of ESA used. Subcutaneous (SC) use is preferable in people who are not receiving HD to avoid puncture of peripheral veins. Subcutaneous administraTon of short-acTng ESA preparaTons may be preferred in people receiving haemodialysis due to improved ESA efficacy at the EPO receptor, allowing for smaller doses to be used. However, convenience and choice may favour intravenous (IV) administraTon in people on HD. (2B)

texts.orig: Guideline 3.9 - Treatment of Anaemia with ESA Therapy - Frequency of Administra9on:
texts.text: Guideline 3.9 - Treatment of Anaemia with ESA Therapy - Frequency of Administra9on:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that the frequency of administraTon should be determined by the CKD grade, person preference, treatment seLng and class of ESA. Less frequent administraTon using long-acTng ESAs may be the treatment of choice in people with CKD not on haemodialysis. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest that the frequency of administraTon should be determined by the CKD grade, person preference, treatment seLng and class of ESA. Less frequent administraTon using long-acTng ESAs may be the treatment of choice in people with CKD not on haemodialysis. (2B)

texts.text: Guideline 3.14 - Monitoring of ESA Treatment - Resistance to ESA Therapy:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that inadequate response ('resistance') to ESA therapy is defined as failure to reach the target Hb range despite SC epoeTn dose >300 IU/kg/week (450 IU/kg/week IV epoeTn), or darbepoeTn dose >1.5 mcg/kg/week, or equivalent dose of methoxy ethylene glycol epoeTn beta following invesTgaTon and treatment of other causes. (1A)

texts.text: We recommend cauTon in people with a history of thromboTc events following the SmPC for contraindicaTons. (1A)

texts.orig: Guideline 4.7.6
texts.text: Guideline 4.7.6

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that HIF-PHI may be considered in people with hyporesponsiveness to ESA therapy or underlying inflammaTon, but further high-quality randomised trials are needed to confirm its effecTveness. (2C)
texts.text: We suggest that HIF-PHI may be considered in people with hyporesponsiveness to ESA therapy or underlying inflammaTon, but further high-quality randomised trials are needed to confirm its effecTveness. (2C)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 4.8 - Safety of HIF-PHI:
texts.text: Guideline 4.8 - Safety of HIF-PHI:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest the cauTous use of HIF-PHI in people with CKD and either known CVD or thromboTc events and consideraTon of lower dose regimes to reduce rapid rises in Hb. (2C)
texts.text: We suggest the cauTous use of HIF-PHI in people with CKD and either known CVD or thromboTc events and consideraTon of lower dose regimes to reduce rapid rises in Hb. (2C)

texts.orig: Guideline 4.9 - Monitoring Response to HIF-PHI:
texts.text: Guideline 4.9 - Monitoring Response to HIF-PHI:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that Hb levels should be monitored every two weeks unTl the desired Hb target range of 100 to 120 g/L is achieved and stabilised, and every 4 weeks thereaVer, or as clinically indicated. (1B)
texts.text: We recommend that Hb levels should be monitored every two weeks unTl the desired Hb target range of 100 to 120 g/L is achieved and stabilised, and every 4 weeks thereaVer, or as clinically indicated. (1B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Treatment of Anaemia of CKD with Blood Transfusions (Guidelines 5.1 - 5.1.3)
texts.text: 5. Treatment of Anaemia of CKD with Blood Transfusions (Guidelines 5.1 - 5.1.3)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 5.1 Blood Transfusion in People with Anaemia of CKD:
texts.text: Guideline 5.1 Blood Transfusion in People with Anaemia of CKD:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that in people with anaemia of CKD, especially those in whom kidney transplantaTon is an opTon, red blood cell transfusion should be avoided if possible, to minimise the risk of allosensiTsaTon. (1A)
texts.text: We recommend that in people with anaemia of CKD, especially those in whom kidney transplantaTon is an opTon, red blood cell transfusion should be avoided if possible, to minimise the risk of allosensiTsaTon. (1A)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 5.1.1
texts.text: Guideline 5.1.1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that if red blood cell transfusion becomes essenTal (usually in the seLng of acute blood loss, acute haemolysis or severe sepsis), transfusion should be based on policies set by local transfusion guidelines rather than Hb target range for ESA therapy in anaemia of CKD. (1B)
texts.text: We recommend that if red blood cell transfusion becomes essenTal (usually in the seLng of acute blood loss, acute haemolysis or severe sepsis), transfusion should be based on policies set by local transfusion guidelines rather than Hb target range for ESA therapy in anaemia of CKD. (1B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 5.1.2
texts.text: Guideline 5.1.2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest using single unit transfusion, where possible, for stable non-bleeding people with CKD who clinically require transfusion. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest using single unit transfusion, where possible, for stable non-bleeding people with CKD who clinically require transfusion. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 5.1.3
texts.text: Guideline 5.1.3

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that kidney transplant recipients, those on the transplant waiTng list or people on immunosuppressive therapy should receive only hepaTTs E negaTve blood components (all UK blood components are tested), but neither CMV negaTve nor irradiated blood is required. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest that kidney transplant recipients, those on the transplant waiTng list or people on immunosuppressive therapy should receive only hepaTTs E negaTve blood components (all UK blood components are tested), but neither CMV negaTve nor irradiated blood is required. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. Management of Peri-Transplant and Post-Transplant Anaemia (Guidelines 6.16.3.3)
texts.text: 6. Management of Peri-Transplant and Post-Transplant Anaemia (Guidelines 6.16.3.3)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 6.1 - Peri-Transplant Anaemia:
texts.text: Guideline 6.1 - Peri-Transplant Anaemia:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that anaemia management should be opTmal pre-transplant in all people with CKD on the transplant wait-list, to minimise the risk of a post-transplant transfusion. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest that anaemia management should be opTmal pre-transplant in all people with CKD on the transplant wait-list, to minimise the risk of a post-transplant transfusion. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 6.1.1
texts.text: Guideline 6.1.1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that for people with CKD undergoing kidney transplantaTon, ESA therapy may be conTnued, aVer a shared decision with the paTent taking account of risks and benefits, unTl endogenous EPO producTon is sufficient to maintain Hb concentraTons. For HIF-PHI therapy we make no recommendaTon unTl further data are available. (2C)
texts.text: We suggest that for people with CKD undergoing kidney transplantaTon, ESA therapy may be conTnued, aVer a shared decision with the paTent taking account of risks and benefits, unTl endogenous EPO producTon is sufficient to maintain Hb concentraTons. For HIF-PHI therapy we make no recommendaTon unTl further data are available. (2C)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 6.2 - Blood Transfusion:
texts.text: Guideline 6.2 - Blood Transfusion:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest for stable non-bleeding individuals who clinically require a red cell transfusion, use single unit transfusion, where possible. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest for stable non-bleeding individuals who clinically require a red cell transfusion, use single unit transfusion, where possible. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 6.3 - Post-Transplant Anaemia:
texts.text: Guideline 6.3 - Post-Transplant Anaemia:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that consideraTon is given to idenTfy (and correct) reversible transplant-specific causes of anaemia. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest that consideraTon is given to idenTfy (and correct) reversible transplant-specific causes of anaemia. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 6.3.1
texts.text: Guideline 6.3.1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that the treatment guidelines for anaemia in kidney transplant recipients should be similar to those for people with CKD not on dialysis (NDD-CKD). (1B)
texts.text: We recommend that the treatment guidelines for anaemia in kidney transplant recipients should be similar to those for people with CKD not on dialysis (NDD-CKD). (1B)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest ESA therapy be considered in people with myelodysplasTc syndrome (MDS), myeloma and others with CKD and haematological disease. We make no recommendaTons on HIF-PHI therapy. (not graded)

texts.text: Guideline 7.3 - Pregnancy:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that pregnant and post-partum women with CKD are given parenteral iron, if indicated, from the second trimester onwards, if the benefit is judged to outweigh the potenTal risk for the mother and fetus and according to SmPC guidance. (1C)
texts.text: We recommend that pregnant and post-partum women with CKD are given parenteral iron, if indicated, from the second trimester onwards, if the benefit is judged to outweigh the potenTal risk for the mother and fetus and according to SmPC guidance. (1C)

texts.text: Guideline 7.3.1

texts.label: text

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 1.1 - Evalua9on of Chronic Anaemia - Screening for Anaemia:
texts.text: Guideline 1.1 - Evalua9on of Chronic Anaemia - Screening for Anaemia:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that haemoglobin (Hb) levels should be rouTnely measured to screen for anaemia:
texts.text: We suggest that haemoglobin (Hb) levels should be rouTnely measured to screen for anaemia:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · At least annually in people with CKD G3
texts.text: At least annually in people with CKD G3

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · At least twice a year in people with CKD G4-5 not on kidney replacement therapy (2B)
texts.text: At least twice a year in people with CKD G4-5 not on kidney replacement therapy (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Rationale
texts.text: Rationale

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There is insufficient literature to suggest the ideal frequency of Hb tesTng in people with CKD who are not on ESA therapy. AlternaTvely, data from clinical trials have shown that the rate of Hb decline in these people is gradual (1, 2) . In a Canadian study to assess the effect of ESA therapy on leV ventricular mass in people with CKD (2) 172 people were assigned either to receive therapy with erythropoieTn α subcutaneously to maintain or achieve Hb level targets of 120 to 140 g/L, or to the control/delayed treatment group with mean Hb levels of 90 ± 5 g/L. During 2 years' follow up a significant proporTon of paTents eventually required ESA therapy. However, among those who did not require ESA therapy, mean Hb values remained relaTvely stable throughout the study period. Hb level should be measured at least monthly in CKD G5 haemodialysis paTents and every three months in CKD G5 peritoneal dialysis paTents.
texts.text: There is insufficient literature to suggest the ideal frequency of Hb tesTng in people with CKD who are not on ESA therapy. AlternaTvely, data from clinical trials have shown that the rate of Hb decline in these people is gradual (1, 2) . In a Canadian study to assess the effect of ESA therapy on leV ventricular mass in people with CKD (2) 172 people were assigned either to receive therapy with erythropoieTn α subcutaneously to maintain or achieve Hb level targets of 120 to 140 g/L, or to the control/delayed treatment group with mean Hb levels of 90 ± 5 g/L. During 2 years' follow up a significant proporTon of paTents eventually required ESA therapy. However, among those who did not require ESA therapy, mean Hb values remained relaTvely stable throughout the study period. Hb level should be measured at least monthly in CKD G5 haemodialysis paTents and every three months in CKD G5 peritoneal dialysis paTents.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: KDIGO 2012 guidelines suggest measurement of Hb at least annually in paTents with NDD-CKD G3, at least twice per year in paTents with NDD-CKD G4-5 and at least every 3 months in paTents with CKD G5HD and CKD G5 PD. For those treated with an ESA, they recommend measuring Hb concentraTon when clinically indicated and at least every 3 months in paTents with NDD-CKD G3-5 and CKD G5 PD and at least monthly in paTents with CKD G5 HD (3) .
texts.text: KDIGO 2012 guidelines suggest measurement of Hb at least annually in paTents with NDD-CKD G3, at least twice per year in paTents with NDD-CKD G4-5 and at least every 3 months in paTents with CKD G5HD and CKD G5 PD. For those treated with an ESA, they recommend measuring Hb concentraTon when clinically indicated and at least every 3 months in paTents with NDD-CKD G3-5 and CKD G5 PD and at least monthly in paTents with CKD G5 HD (3) .

texts.orig: We recommend that all people with chronic anaemia associated with chronic kidney disease should be invesTgated for the cause and possible treatment, irrespecTve of the grade of kidney disease or requirement for kidney replacement therapy if:

texts.text: Their haemoglobin (Hb) levels are less than 110 g/L (less than 105 g/L if younger than 2 years), or they develop symptoms aXributable to anaemia. This is to ensure the correct diagnosis and management of anaemia. (1A)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Rationale
texts.text: Rationale

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The UK Kidney AssociaTon (UKKA) and Royal College of Physicians endorse the NICE Guidelines for Chronic Kidney Disease: Managing Anaemia (1) . The reader is referred to these guidelines as well as the European Renal Best PracTce (ERBP) for Anaemia in CKD (2,3) and the KDOQI (4) Guidelines for management of anaemia in CKD. The KDIGO website (www.kdigo.org) (5) is a useful site of reference for comparison of evidence-based guidelines internaTonally.
texts.text: The UK Kidney AssociaTon (UKKA) and Royal College of Physicians endorse the NICE Guidelines for Chronic Kidney Disease: Managing Anaemia (1) . The reader is referred to these guidelines as well as the European Renal Best PracTce (ERBP) for Anaemia in CKD (2,3) and the KDOQI (4) Guidelines for management of anaemia in CKD. The KDIGO website (www.kdigo.org) (5) is a useful site of reference for comparison of evidence-based guidelines internaTonally.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Anaemia is defined as having a Hb value below the established cut off defined by the World Health OrganisaTon (4) . Different defined groups have different cut offs. For adults:
texts.text: Anaemia is defined as having a Hb value below the established cut off defined by the World Health OrganisaTon (4) . Different defined groups have different cut offs. For adults:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Men and postmenopausal women Hb <130 g/L
texts.text: Men and postmenopausal women Hb <130 g/L

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Premenopausal women Hb <120 g/L
texts.text: Premenopausal women Hb <120 g/L

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Pregnant women Hb <110 g/L
texts.text: Pregnant women Hb <110 g/L

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In 2006, KDOQI modified this definiTon by giving a single criterion for diagnosing anaemia in adult males (Hb < 135 g/L, regardless of age) because the decrease in Hb among males aged > 60 years is oVen aXributable to associated co-morbidiTes (4), while KDIGO suggest a diagnosis of anaemia in adults with CKD when the Hb concentraTon is <130 g/L in males and <120 g/L in females (5) .
texts.text: In 2006, KDOQI modified this definiTon by giving a single criterion for diagnosing anaemia in adult males (Hb < 135 g/L, regardless of age) because the decrease in Hb among males aged > 60 years is oVen aXributable to associated co-morbidiTes (4), while KDIGO suggest a diagnosis of anaemia in adults with CKD when the Hb concentraTon is <130 g/L in males and <120 g/L in females (5) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Anaemia is defined as a haemoglobin concentraTon less than the 5th percenTle for age. Hb levels vary by age, and many laboratories use adult norms as references; therefore, the paTent's Hb level must be compared with age-based norms to diagnose anaemia (6) .
texts.text: Anaemia is defined as a haemoglobin concentraTon less than the 5th percenTle for age. Hb levels vary by age, and many laboratories use adult norms as references; therefore, the paTent's Hb level must be compared with age-based norms to diagnose anaemia (6) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addiTon to sex, age and pregnancy other factors influence Hb level including smoking, alTtude, race and geneTc disorders (thalassemia and sickle cell disease). In CKD, a person's anaemia should be defined using these same criteria. The degree of kidney impairment affects the likelihood of any person developing anaemia. Although current treatment with ESAs is not recommended unless Hb falls consistently below 110 g/L, other causes of anaemia should be excluded in persons with Hb below normal range. The current definiTon for anaemia applies to adult subjects older than 18 years, of all races and ethnic groups, and living at relaTvely low alTtude (<1,000 m or 3,000 V.) (7) . With increasing alTtude, endogenous erythropoieTn producTon is increased; as a result, Hb concentraTon can be expected to increase by about 6 g/L in women and 9 g/L in men for each 1,000 m of alTtude above sea level (8) .
texts.text: In addiTon to sex, age and pregnancy other factors influence Hb level including smoking, alTtude, race and geneTc disorders (thalassemia and sickle cell disease). Although current treatment with ESAs is not recommended unless Hb falls consistently below 110 g/L, other causes of anaemia should be excluded in persons with Hb below normal range. The current definiTon for anaemia applies to adult subjects older than 18 years, of all races and ethnic groups, and living at relaTvely low alTtude (<1,000 m or 3,000 V.) (7) . With increasing alTtude, endogenous erythropoieTn producTon is increased; as a result, Hb concentraTon can be expected to increase by about 6 g/L in women and 9 g/L in men for each 1,000 m of alTtude above sea level (8) .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 1.3 - Evalua9on of Anaemia - Kidney Func9on:
texts.text: Guideline 1.3 - Evalua9on of Anaemia - Kidney Func9on:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that CKD should be considered as a possible cause of anaemia when the glomerular filtraTon rate (GFR) is < 60 ml/min/1.73m 2 . It is more likely to be the cause if the GFR is <30ml/min/1.73m 2 (< 45 ml/min/ 1.73m 2 in people with diabetes) and no other cause, e.g., blood loss, folic acid or vitamin B12 deficiency, is idenTfied. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest that CKD should be considered as a possible cause of anaemia when the glomerular filtraTon rate (GFR) is < 60 ml/min/1.73m 2 . It is more likely to be the cause if the GFR is <30ml/min/1.73m 2 (< 45 ml/min/ 1.73m 2 in people with diabetes) and no other cause, e.g., blood loss, folic acid or vitamin B12 deficiency, is idenTfied. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Rationale
texts.text: Rationale

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The prevalence of anaemia in people with NDD-CKD increases as the GFR progressively falls (1) . NHANES III data demonstrate a prevalence of anaemia of 1%, 9% and 33% in people with NDD-CKD with an eGFR of 60, 30 and 15 ml/min/1.73m 2 respecTvely (2) . UK data of > 112,000 unselected paTents in the general populaTon showed a populaTon prevalence of NDD-CKD G3-G5 of 4.9% (3) . In these people the prevalence of sex specific anaemia (<120 g/L men: < 110 g/L women) was 12%.
texts.text: The prevalence of anaemia in people with NDD-CKD increases as the GFR progressively falls (1) . NHANES III data demonstrate a prevalence of anaemia of 1%, 9% and 33% in people with NDD-CKD with an eGFR of 60, 30 and 15 ml/min/1.73m 2 respecTvely (2) . UK data of > 112,000 unselected paTents in the general populaTon showed a populaTon prevalence of NDD-CKD G3-G5 of 4.9% (3) . In these people the prevalence of sex specific anaemia (<120 g/L men: < 110 g/L women) was 12%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Anaemia is more prevalent among people with diabetes. In addiTon, anaemia of CKD develops earlier in people with diabetes compared with people who do not have diabetes (4-8) . In a crosssecTonal study involving over 800 people with diabetes, anaemia has been found to be two to three Tmes more prevalent in paTents with diabetes compared with the general populaTon at all levels of GFR (9) .

texts.text: Guideline 1.4 - Evalua9on of Anaemia - Erythropoie9n Measurement:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that measurement of erythropoieTn levels should NOT rouTnely be considered for the diagnosis or management of anaemia for people with CKD. (1A)
texts.text: We recommend that measurement of erythropoieTn levels should NOT rouTnely be considered for the diagnosis or management of anaemia for people with CKD. (1A)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Rationale
texts.text: Rationale

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In kidney anaemia, serum erythropoieTn (EPO) levels are lower than appropriate for the degree of anaemia. In people with CKD and anaemia, erythropoieTn Ttres are not lower but may be equal to or even higher than in normal non-anaemic individuals (1-3) . Measurement of erythropoieTn level is very rarely helpful.

In people with CKD and anaemia, erythropoieTn Ttres are not lower but may be equal to or even higher than in normal non-anaemic individuals (1-3) . Measurement of erythropoieTn level is very rarely helpful.

texts.orig: Although relaTve erythropoieTn deficiency is common among people with anaemia and CKD, other potenTal causes should be idenTfied or excluded. A clinical and laboratory evaluaTon of the cause of anaemia should precede iniTaTon of ESA therapy. The recommended laboratory evaluaTon aims at assessing:
texts.text: Although relaTve erythropoieTn deficiency is common among people with anaemia and CKD, other potenTal causes should be idenTfied or excluded. A clinical and laboratory evaluaTon of the cause of anaemia should precede iniTaTon of ESA therapy. The recommended laboratory evaluaTon aims at assessing:

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a) Assessment of anaemia severity
texts.text: a) Assessment of anaemia severity

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In people with CKD not yet requiring dialysis and in those on peritoneal dialysis (PD), the Tming of the blood sample draw is not criTcal because plasma volume in these people remains relaTvely constant. In people receiving haemodialysis (HD) one issue remains to be clarified. Haemoglobin concentraTons are rouTnely measured in people receiving dialysis before dialysis. This potenTally leads to lower haematocrit values as a result of diluTon from fluid overload prior to ultrafiltraTon and an underesTmate to actual haemoglobin value.
texts.text: In people with CKD not yet requiring dialysis and in those on peritoneal dialysis (PD), the Tming of the blood sample draw is not criTcal because plasma volume in these people remains relaTvely constant. In people receiving haemodialysis (HD) one issue remains to be clarified. Haemoglobin concentraTons are rouTnely measured in people receiving dialysis before dialysis. This potenTally leads to lower haematocrit values as a result of diluTon from fluid overload prior to ultrafiltraTon and an underesTmate to actual haemoglobin value.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: leads to an increase in Hb level. Thus, a pre-dialysis sample underesTmates the euvolaemic Hb level, whereas a post dialysis sample over-esTmates the euvolaemic Hb. Indeed, changes on haematocrit can vary from the start to the end of dialysis by up to 6% depending on the volume of ultrafiltraTon. In a study of 68 stable people receiving HD receiving erythropoieTn subcutaneously, average mean pre-dialysis Hb was 10 g/L lower than average post dialysis Hb (1) . There was a strong linear inverse correlaTon between percentage of change in Hb and haematocrit (Hct) values and percentage of change in body weight. In another study of 49 stable HD paTents, among all pre-HD and post-HD Hb values, levels measured at the end of short dialysis intervals were closest to the mean Hb value of the week, derived from calculaTon of the area under the curve for the readings of the week (2) . In unit-based haemodialysis persons receiving thrice weekly dialysis, Hb monitoring performed prior to a mid-week haemodialysis session would minimise Hb variability due to the longer inter-dialyTc interval between the last treatment of one week and the first of the next.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Serum ferriTn level is the only available blood marker of storage iron. There are several tests to assess adequacy of iron for erythropoiesis: TSAT, MCV, MCH, percentage of hypochromic red blood cells (HRC) and reTculocyte Hb content (CHr) or RET-He. Serum iron has not rouTnely been studied in people with CKD.

texts.orig: · HRC esTmaTon is a useful test for assessment of iron availability but is limited by the effect of sample storage Tme and need for special analysers. Long sample storage Tme (> 6 hours) may spuriously increase HRC. Because a fresh sample is needed, this measure may not be pracTcal in rouTne clinical pracTce, especially in satellite dialysis paTents.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Historically in people with NDD-CKD, it was proposed that serum ferriTn levels less than 25 ng/mL in males and less than 12 ng/mL in females were suggesTve of depleTon of iron stores as a cause of anaemia; but serum ferriTn level is less reliable in the evaluaTon of iron stores in people with NDDCKD and in those receiving HD, because ferriTn level is affected by other factors in addiTon to iron storage status. In relaTvely healthy people receiving HD, before widespread use of IV iron therapy, the finding of a ferriTn level less than 50 ng/mL was not uncommon (1) and was associated with absent bone marrow iron in approximately 80% of people (2) . However, in people receiving HD with several co-morbidiTes, absent iron stores may sTll be found at ferriTn levels approaching or even exceeding 200 ng/mL (3) .

Nevertheless, given the high prevalence of GI blood loss due to variety of causes in this populaTon, deciding on a subsequent management plan, including endoscopy, depends on the clinical presentaTon. This supports the recommendaTon that people with CKD who present with anaemia and iron deficiency should undergo careful clinical assessment prior to the iniTaTon of anaemia therapy (5-7) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Increasing the Hb in anaemic people with CKD places the greatest demand for iron in the erythropoieTc Tssues. During ESA or HIF-PHI inducTon therapy iron requirements will depend on the rate of erythropoiesis, the Hb deficit, and ongoing iron losses. Once the target Hb has been reached and Hb stabilised, the iron requirements will be dependent on ongoing iron losses. When adequate iron status is achieved, those on ESA or HIF-PHI therapy should be given maintenance iron treatment as required.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several studies have reported that the dose of ESA required to achieve and maintain a given Hb level is inversely related to iron stores (2-7) . Iron deficiency (absolute or funcTonal) was the main cause of ESA resistance in the UK, but this has now been solved by parenteral iron replacement strategies (8) . The evidence behind the statement that TSAT generally should be maintained at greater than 20% stems from a single RCT comparing higher to lower TSAT targets; parTcipants randomised to a target TSAT of 30% to 50% demonstrated a 40% reducTon in ESA dose compared with those assigned to a target of 20% to 30% (9) .

Iron deficiency (absolute or funcTonal) was the main cause of ESA resistance in the UK, but this has now been solved by parenteral iron replacement strategies (8) . The evidence behind the statement that TSAT generally should be maintained at greater than 20% stems from a single RCT comparing higher to lower TSAT targets; parTcipants randomised to a target TSAT of 30% to 50% demonstrated a 40% reducTon in ESA dose compared with those assigned to a target of 20% to 30% (9) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a randomised controlled study involving 157 haemodialysis parTcipants comparing iron management based on serum ferriTn and transferrin saturaTon versus CHr, CHr was a markedly more stable analyte than serum ferriTn or transferrin saturaTon. Iron management based on CHr resulted in similar haematocrit and epoeTn dosing while significantly reducing IV iron exposure (10) . In another study involving 164 people with CKD on HD, low CHr (<26 pg) was suggesTve of funcTonal iron deficiency. When a subgroup of parTcipants were randomly assigned to receive a single dose of IV iron dextran (1000 mg), a CHr <26 pg at baseline predicted iron deficiency with a sensiTvity of 100% and specificity of 80%. The serum ferriTn, transferrin saturaTon and percentage of hypochromic red blood cells were all less accurate. The Tme to correcTon of iron deficiency at the level of the reTculocyte was found to be within 48 hours as measured by correcTon of the mean CHr to >26 pg, and by the shiV of the majority of the reTculocyte populaTon to CHr >26 pg within this Tme span (11) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: TSAT and serum ferriTn were evaluated in 47 people with HD with baseline serum ferriTn levels <600 mcg/L. ParTcipants were treated with IV dextran (1000 mg over ten haemodialysis treatments). ParTcipants were classified as having iron deficiency if haematocrit value increased by 5% or if their erythropoieTn dose decreased by 10% by 2 months. Receiver operator curves (ROC) demonstrated that none of the iron indices had a high level of uTlity (both sensiTvity and specificity >80%). As such it was concluded that both tests should be interpreted in the context of the parTcipant's underlying EPO responsiveness. In parTcipants who are responsive to EPO, a TSAT <18% or serum ferriTn level <100 mcg/L should be used to indicate inadequate iron. When EPO resistance is present, TSAT<27% or ferriTn <300 mcg/L should be used to guide iron management (15) .

texts.orig: A study of 209 parTcipants with solid tumours or haematological malignancy using a threshold of CHr 32 pg ruled out iron deficiency with a negaTve predicTve value of 98.5% (16) . An American study of 556 parTcipants who were referred for anaemia management yielded a receiver operaTng characterisTc analysis CHr cut-off of 30.7 pg to idenTfy IDA with 68.2% sensiTvity and 69.7% specificity (17) . A further study of 40 people with CKD-HD in the maintenance phase of erythropoieTn therapy compared for each person: baseline aVer an iron-free period and a second sample aVer 4 weeks of IV iron. For a CHr cut-off of 30.8 pg the area under curve (AUC) was 0.84 (95% CI 0.640.93), sensiTvity 78.7%, and specificity 87.2%. %HRC AUC was 0.78 (95% CI 0.64-0.91), at cutoff 2.4%, sensiTvity 72.2%, and specificity 88.1% (18) .

texts.orig: NICE guidelines on the evaluaTon of iron therapy in people with CKD suggests that for people on haemodialysis, %HRC >6% dominated all other iron evaluaTon strategies (it led both to more QALYs and lower cost) (1) . For the other people, TSAT<20% alone or serum ferriTn <100 mcg/L alone were the least cost-effecTve strategy, but %HRC was the most cost-effecTve (1) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Use percentage of hypochromic red blood cells (% HRC; > 6%), but only if processing of blood sample is possible within 6 hours. Since a fresh blood sample is needed, this test may be difficult to use rouTnely in clinical pracTce.
texts.text: Use percentage of hypochromic red blood cells (% HRC; > 6%), but only if processing of blood sample is possible within 6 hours. Since a fresh blood sample is needed, this test may be difficult to use rouTnely in clinical pracTce.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · If using percentage of hypochromic red blood cells is not possible, use reTculocyte Hb content (CHr <31 pg- revised in these guidelines but NICE remains currently at 29 pg) or equivalent tests - for example, reTculocyte Hb equivalent.
texts.text: If using percentage of hypochromic red blood cells is not possible, use reTculocyte Hb content (CHr <31 pg- revised in these guidelines but NICE remains currently at 29 pg) or equivalent tests - for example, reTculocyte Hb equivalent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We believe that CHr (<31 pg) is more sensiTve in determining iron depleTon than %HRC (17-19) . This is because CHr reflects haemoglobin content of young reTculocytes, and therefore reflects iron availability in the preceding few days; while %HRC reflects haemoglobin contents of whole erythrocyte pool, and since senescent erythrocyte tend to get smaller in volume, the test may be affected by the overall rate of erythropoiesis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that ESA or HIF-PHI therapy should not be iniTated in the presence of absolute iron deficiency (ferriTn <100 mcg/L in NDD-CKD and <200 mcg/L in DD-CKD) or funcTonal Iron deficiency (TSAT <20% will normal or elevated ferriTn levels) unTl this is corrected and anaemia persists. Iron supplements should be given prior to or when iniTaTng ESA or HIF-PHI therapy. (1B)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A consistent finding in the series of trials using HIF-PHI (Roxadustat) was the effect on iron metabolism. In the non-dialysis trials against placebo (ANDES, ALPS and OLYMPUS), there was a 50% reducTon in the requirement for IV iron use, such that the monthly dose of iron required was significantly reduced (2-4) . This led to reducTons in serum ferriTn but no change in TSAT or serum iron. However, the trials did not assess the quanTty of oral iron use. This suggests that HIF-PHI therapy may simultaneously correct anaemia and iron depleTon. In the DOLOMITES trial, the use of either IV or oral iron was also reduced in the Roxadustat vs darbepoeTn alfa groups. While iron administraTon was leV to the invesTgator's discreTon and/or were based on local clinical pracTce paXerns in the darbepoeTn alfa group, oral iron was requested by trial protocol in the Roxadustat group (5) .
texts.text: A consistent finding in the series of trials using HIF-PHI (Roxadustat) was the effect on iron metabolism. In the non-dialysis trials against placebo (ANDES, ALPS and OLYMPUS), there was a 50% reducTon in the requirement for IV iron use, such that the monthly dose of iron required was significantly reduced (2-4) . This led to reducTons in serum ferriTn but no change in TSAT or serum iron. However, the trials did not assess the quanTty of oral iron use. This suggests that HIF-PHI therapy may simultaneously correct anaemia and iron depleTon. In the DOLOMITES trial, the use of either IV or oral iron was also reduced in the Roxadustat vs darbepoeTn alfa groups. While iron administraTon was leV to the invesTgator's discreTon and/or were based on local clinical pracTce paXerns in the darbepoeTn alfa group, oral iron was requested by trial protocol in the Roxadustat group (5) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another challenge in assessing the impact of HIF-PHI on iron use was that in some trials, iron protocols differed between treatment and comparator groups within a trial while other trials tested similar iron protocols (6, 7, 8) . Other potenTal trial design limitaTons included differences in Hb targets and the actual achieved Hb between treatment arms, differences in the proporTon of parTcipants with baseline iron deficiency and baseline imbalances in iron and hepcidin status and potenTally relevant co-morbidiTes. Therefore, further data from randomised controlled trials is needed to confirm these secondary findings on iron uTlisaTon before definiTve guidance can be given.
texts.text: Another challenge in assessing the impact of HIF-PHI on iron use was that in some trials, iron protocols differed between treatment and comparator groups within a trial while other trials tested similar iron protocols (6, 7, 8) . Other potenTal trial design limitaTons included differences in Hb targets and the actual achieved Hb between treatment arms, differences in the proporTon of parTcipants with baseline iron deficiency and baseline imbalances in iron and hepcidin status and potenTally relevant co-morbidiTes. Therefore, further data from randomised controlled trials is needed to confirm these secondary findings on iron uTlisaTon before definiTve guidance can be given.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Iron parameters should be monitored during treatment with HIF-PHI therapy and iron deficiency should be avoided because it is associated with thromboembolic events, impaired red blood cell producTon (9) , lower health related quality of life (HRQoL), higher rates of cardiovascular events and higher mortality (10) .

texts.orig: We suggest that for people with CKD not requiring HD, or people currently receiving PD who are being considered for ESA therapy, intravenous iron should be considered to reduce ESA dose requirements. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest that for people with CKD not requiring HD, or people currently receiving PD who are being considered for ESA therapy, intravenous iron should be considered to reduce ESA dose requirements. (2B)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest for people with NDD-CKD or maintained on PD (i.e. not requiring HD), the choice between oral vs. parenteral iron depends on a shared decision and should include the impact of the severity of iron deficiency, the previous response and side effects, the availability of venous access and the need to iniTate ESA or HIF-PHI therapy. (2A)

(2A)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Rationale
texts.text: Rationale

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The evidence base for IV iron over oral iron in people with CKD who are pre-dialysis or maintained on PD is limited. Oral iron, if tolerated, appears to be adequate in most people parTcularly in combinaTon with ESA therapy. In people who appear resistant to ESA therapy on oral iron, or are intolerant of oral iron, a therapeuTc trial of IV iron trial seems reasonable.
texts.text: The evidence base for IV iron over oral iron in people with CKD who are pre-dialysis or maintained on PD is limited. Oral iron, if tolerated, appears to be adequate in most people parTcularly in combinaTon with ESA therapy. In people who appear resistant to ESA therapy on oral iron, or are intolerant of oral iron, a therapeuTc trial of IV iron trial seems reasonable.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · One randomised study in 188 parTcipants with IV iron (1000 mg iron sucrose in divided doses over 14 days) versus oral iron (ferrous sulphate 325 mg TDS) in pre-dialysis parTcipants demonstrated a greater improvement in Hb outcome in those on IV iron (more parTcipants achieved a Hb increased of >10 g/L) but no difference in the proporTon of parTcipants who had to commence ESA aVer the start of the study (1).
texts.text: One randomised study in 188 parTcipants with IV iron (1000 mg iron sucrose in divided doses over 14 days) versus oral iron (ferrous sulphate 325 mg TDS) in pre-dialysis parTcipants demonstrated a greater improvement in Hb outcome in those on IV iron (more parTcipants achieved a Hb increased of >10 g/L) but no difference in the proporTon of parTcipants who had to commence ESA aVer the start of the study (1).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Two studies in pre-dialysis populaTons not on ESA (one without oral iron and the other aVer oral iron therapy) demonstrated improvements in Hb outcome aVer IV iron (2, 3) .
texts.text: Two studies in pre-dialysis populaTons not on ESA (one without oral iron and the other aVer oral iron therapy) demonstrated improvements in Hb outcome aVer IV iron (2, 3) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Oral iron is easy and cheap to prescribe. However, it seems reasonable to treat people with CKD who have not responded to, or have been intolerant of, oral iron with IV iron.
texts.text: Oral iron is easy and cheap to prescribe. However, it seems reasonable to treat people with CKD who have not responded to, or have been intolerant of, oral iron with IV iron.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two randomised controlled trials of oral versus IV iron supplementaTon in parTcipants with advanced NDD-CKD receiving concomitant ESAs agree. In the first study of 45 parTcipants with Hb <110 g/L given either ferrous sulphate 200 mg TDS versus 300 mg iron sucrose IV monthly, there was no difference in Hb or ESA dose between the oral and IV group receiving ESA over a mean 5.2 months follow-up (4) . Iron stores were greater in the IV than oral group. Five parTcipants (55%) in the oral iron group had diabetes, compared to none on the IV iron group and this may have confounded the results on iron stores. In addiTon, more parTcipants in the oral iron group were exposed to ACEi/ARBs.
texts.text: Two randomised controlled trials of oral versus IV iron supplementaTon in parTcipants with advanced NDD-CKD receiving concomitant ESAs agree. In the first study of 45 parTcipants with Hb <110 g/L given either ferrous sulphate 200 mg TDS versus 300 mg iron sucrose IV monthly, there was no difference in Hb or ESA dose between the oral and IV group receiving ESA over a mean 5.2 months follow-up (4) . Iron stores were greater in the IV than oral group. Five parTcipants (55%) in the oral iron group had diabetes, compared to none on the IV iron group and this may have confounded the results on iron stores. In addiTon, more parTcipants in the oral iron group were exposed to ACEi/ARBs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Similar findings were reported in another study of 96 people with NDD-CKD comparing 5 weeks of IV iron sucrose (200 mg every 7 days for a total of 5 doses) versus 29 days of thrice daily oral iron (ferrous sulphate 325 mg TDS). There was no difference in Hb or ESA dose but greater increase in ferriTn in the IV group (5) . In this study the frequency of gastrointesTnal symptoms was greater in the oral iron group than the IV iron group (consTpaTon 34.5% vs. 12.5%; nausea 10.4% vs. 4.2%). High dose, low frequency (HiD/LF) is considered to be a maximum of 2 infusions. For adults this is considered to be a minimum of 500 mg of iron in each infusion. Low dose, high frequency (LD/HF) is considered to be more than 2 infusions. For adults, there would typically be 100-200 mg of iron in each infusion.
texts.text: Similar findings were reported in another study of 96 people with NDD-CKD comparing 5 weeks of IV iron sucrose (200 mg every 7 days for a total of 5 doses) versus 29 days of thrice daily oral iron (ferrous sulphate 325 mg TDS). There was no difference in Hb or ESA dose but greater increase in ferriTn in the IV group (5) . In this study the frequency of gastrointesTnal symptoms was greater in the oral iron group than the IV iron group (consTpaTon 34.5% vs. 12.5%; nausea 10.4% vs. 4.2%). High dose, low frequency (HiD/LF) is considered to be a maximum of 2 infusions. For adults this is considered to be a minimum of 500 mg of iron in each infusion. Low dose, high frequency (LD/HF) is considered to be more than 2 infusions. For adults, there would typically be 100-200 mg of iron in each infusion.
