texts.label: text
texts.orig: There is increasing evidence that using IV iron in CKD-PD is safe, improves Hb levels and reduces ESA dose requirements (6) :
texts.text: There is increasing evidence that using IV iron in CKD-PD is safe, improves Hb levels and reduces ESA dose requirements (6) :

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · A cross-over study of oral and IV iron demonstrated higher Hb and lower ESA doses with IV iron aVer 4 months oral (7) .
texts.text: A cross-over study of oral and IV iron demonstrated higher Hb and lower ESA doses with IV iron aVer 4 months oral (7) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · A retrospecTve study evaluaTng ferric carboxymaltose (FCM) in a populaTon of 91 people with CKD-PD over 12 months found no SAEs including hypersensiTvity. More than 60% of parTcipants achieved ferriTn 200-800 mcg/L and TSAT >20% and a reduced ESA dose was necessary to maintain Hb levels (8) .
texts.text: A retrospecTve study evaluaTng ferric carboxymaltose (FCM) in a populaTon of 91 people with CKD-PD over 12 months found no SAEs including hypersensiTvity. More than 60% of parTcipants achieved ferriTn 200-800 mcg/L and TSAT >20% and a reduced ESA dose was necessary to maintain Hb levels (8) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · A study demonstrated that abnormal iron status, is associated with an increased risk of mortality in people treated with PD. FuncTonal Iron Deficiency (FID) had the greatest all-cause and cardiovascular mortality (9) .
texts.text: A study demonstrated that abnormal iron status, is associated with an increased risk of mortality in people treated with PD. FuncTonal Iron Deficiency (FID) had the greatest all-cause and cardiovascular mortality (9) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The relaTve safety of parenteral iron compared with oral iron was assessed in a study involving parTcipants with stage 3 and 4 NDD-CKD and iron deficiency anaemia. Study parTcipants were randomly assigned to either oral ferrous sulphate (n=69 to 325 mg three Tmes daily for 8 weeks) or IV iron sucrose (n=67 to 200 mg every 2 weeks, total 1 g). The trial was terminated early based of a higher risk of serious adverse events in the IV iron treatment group. There were 36 serious cardiovascular events among 19 parTcipants assigned to the oral iron treatment group and 55 events among 17 parTcipants of the intravenous iron group (adjusted incidence rate raTo 2.51 (1.56-4.04)). InfecTons resulTng in hospitalisaTon had a significantly increased adjusted incidence rate raTo of 2.12 (1.24-3.64). The authors concluded that among people with NDD-CKD and anaemia, IV iron therapy could be associated with an increased risk of serious adverse events, including those from cardiovascular causes and infecTous diseases (10) .
texts.text: The relaTve safety of parenteral iron compared with oral iron was assessed in a study involving parTcipants with stage 3 and 4 NDD-CKD and iron deficiency anaemia. Study parTcipants were randomly assigned to either oral ferrous sulphate (n=69 to 325 mg three Tmes daily for 8 weeks) or IV iron sucrose (n=67 to 200 mg every 2 weeks, total 1 g). The trial was terminated early based of a higher risk of serious adverse events in the IV iron treatment group. There were 36 serious cardiovascular events among 19 parTcipants assigned to the oral iron treatment group and 55 events among 17 parTcipants of the intravenous iron group (adjusted incidence rate raTo 2.51 (1.56-4.04)). InfecTons resulTng in hospitalisaTon had a significantly increased adjusted incidence rate raTo of 2.12 (1.24-3.64). The authors concluded that among people with NDD-CKD and anaemia, IV iron therapy could be associated with an increased risk of serious adverse events, including those from cardiovascular causes and infecTous diseases (10) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Conversely, the above finding was not reproduced in another larger trial that involved 626 people with NDD-CKD with anaemia and iron deficiency not on ESAs (11) . In this trial, parTcipants were randomised (1:1:2) to intravenous (IV) ferric carboxymaltose (FCM), targeTng a higher (400-600 microgram/L) or lower (100-200 microgram/L) ferriTn range or oral iron therapy. The primary end point was Tme to iniTaTon of other anaemia management (ESA, other iron therapy or blood transfusion) or Hb trigger of two consecuTve values <100 g/L during Weeks 8-52. Achieving a higher ferriTn target range led to a delay in the need to iniTate ESA therapy and also a reducTon in ESA dosage. The increase in Hb was greater with high-ferriTn FCM versus oral iron (P = 0.014) and a greater proporTon of parTcipants achieved an Hb increase ≥10 g/L with high-ferriTn FCM versus oral iron (HR: 2.04; 95% CI: 1.52-2.72; P < 0.001). Rates of adverse events and serious adverse events were similar in all groups (11) .
texts.text: Conversely, the above finding was not reproduced in another larger trial that involved 626 people with NDD-CKD with anaemia and iron deficiency not on ESAs (11) . In this trial, parTcipants were randomised (1:1:2) to intravenous (IV) ferric carboxymaltose (FCM), targeTng a higher (400-600 microgram/L) or lower (100-200 microgram/L) ferriTn range or oral iron therapy. The primary end point was Tme to iniTaTon of other anaemia management (ESA, other iron therapy or blood transfusion) or Hb trigger of two consecuTve values <100 g/L during Weeks 8-52. Achieving a higher ferriTn target range led to a delay in the need to iniTate ESA therapy and also a reducTon in ESA dosage. The increase in Hb was greater with high-ferriTn FCM versus oral iron (P = 0.014) and a greater proporTon of parTcipants achieved an Hb increase ≥10 g/L with high-ferriTn FCM versus oral iron (HR: 2.04; 95% CI: 1.52-2.72; P < 0.001). Rates of adverse events and serious adverse events were similar in all groups (11) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Similarly, no safety signal could be detected in another trial comparing intravenous iron isomaltoside versus oral iron in stage G5 NDD-CKD. In this trial 351 iron-deficient parTcipants were randomised 2:1 to either iron isomaltoside 1000 or iron sulphate administered as 100 mg elemental oral iron twice daily (200 mg daily) for 8 weeks. Hb response, serum-ferriTn and TSAT were significantly increased with IV iron compared with those treated with oral iron. Incidence of adverse drug reacTons was not different between both groups. More parTcipants treated with oral iron sulphate withdrew from the study due to adverse events (4.3 versus 0.9%, P = 0.2) (12) .
texts.text: Similarly, no safety signal could be detected in another trial comparing intravenous iron isomaltoside versus oral iron in stage G5 NDD-CKD. In this trial 351 iron-deficient parTcipants were randomised 2:1 to either iron isomaltoside 1000 or iron sulphate administered as 100 mg elemental oral iron twice daily (200 mg daily) for 8 weeks. Hb response, serum-ferriTn and TSAT were significantly increased with IV iron compared with those treated with oral iron. Incidence of adverse drug reacTons was not different between both groups. More parTcipants treated with oral iron sulphate withdrew from the study due to adverse events (4.3 versus 0.9%, P = 0.2) (12) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At present oral iron should remain first line treatment among people with NDD-CKD and IV iron used if recipients are intolerant of oral iron or remain with absolute or FID despite oral iron therapy. The further interpretaTon of these results is limited by several factors including the relaTve short duraTon of follow-up and limited data on potenTal long term adverse effects such as the impact of oxidaTve stress. However, as indicated by NICE, therapy should be individualised, or perhaps tailored and a high dose low frequency intravenous regime should be employed.
texts.text: At present oral iron should remain first line treatment among people with NDD-CKD and IV iron used if recipients are intolerant of oral iron or remain with absolute or FID despite oral iron therapy. The further interpretaTon of these results is limited by several factors including the relaTve short duraTon of follow-up and limited data on potenTal long term adverse effects such as the impact of oxidaTve stress. However, as indicated by NICE, therapy should be individualised, or perhaps tailored and a high dose low frequency intravenous regime should be employed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: People treated with HD have addiTonal iron losses from GI bleeding, blood tests and losses in the dialysis circuit that result in iron supplementaTon requirements that outstrip the capacity of the gut to absorb iron effecTvely. Maintenance IV iron in HD greatly reduces ESA requirements and costs (1,4,13-18) . Maintaining iron stores at steady state in a HD populaTon requires 50-60 mg/week of intravenous iron (14-16) . How this is repleted remains a subject under study. A recent open label, randomised, mulTcentre, non-inferiority trial conducted in 351 people on HD randomised 2 : 1 to either iron isomaltoside 1000 (Group A) or iron sucrose (Group B) (19) . Subjects in Group A were equally divided into A1 (500 mg single bolus injecTon) and A2 (500 mg split dose). Group B were also treated with 500 mg split dose. All treatments showed similar efficacy and safety (19) .
texts.text: People treated with HD have addiTonal iron losses from GI bleeding, blood tests and losses in the dialysis circuit that result in iron supplementaTon requirements that outstrip the capacity of the gut to absorb iron effecTvely. Maintenance IV iron in HD greatly reduces ESA requirements and costs (1,4,13-18) . Maintaining iron stores at steady state in a HD populaTon requires 50-60 mg/week of intravenous iron (14-16) . How this is repleted remains a subject under study. A recent open label, randomised, mulTcentre, non-inferiority trial conducted in 351 people on HD randomised 2 : 1 to either iron isomaltoside 1000 (Group A) or iron sucrose (Group B) (19) . Subjects in Group A were equally divided into A1 (500 mg single bolus injecTon) and A2 (500 mg split dose). Group B were also treated with 500 mg split dose. All treatments showed similar efficacy and safety (19) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The FERWON Trial of 1538 people with NDD-CKD and iron deficiency anaemia (Hb <110 g/L, SF <100 mcg/L or <300 mcg/L and TSAT<30% and stable dose of ESA, was a randomised, open label study. 1027 parTcipants were randomised to 1g iron isomaltoside and 511 to iron sucrose (200 mg given in in total up to five Tme) (20) . The co-primary endpoints were serious or severe hypersensiTvity reacTons and change in Hb from baseline to week 8. There were addiTonal key safety endpoints:  the incidence of composite cardiovascular adverse events and frequency of hypophosphatemia (<0.65 mmol/L]). The results showed a faster and greater early response with iron isomaltoside for Hb, SF and TSAT compared to iron sucrose. There was no staTsTcally significant difference in the frequency of serious or severe hypersensiTvity reacTons between the treatment groups. The incidence of composite cardiovascular AEs was staTsTcally significantly lower in the isomaltoside group compared to the sucrose group (4.1% vs 6.9%; p=0.25).
texts.text: The FERWON Trial of 1538 people with NDD-CKD and iron deficiency anaemia (Hb <110 g/L, SF <100 mcg/L or <300 mcg/L and TSAT<30% and stable dose of ESA, was a randomised, open label study. 1027 parTcipants were randomised to 1g iron isomaltoside and 511 to iron sucrose (200 mg given in in total up to five Tme) (20) . The co-primary endpoints were serious or severe hypersensiTvity reacTons and change in Hb from baseline to week 8. There were addiTonal key safety endpoints:  the incidence of composite cardiovascular adverse events and frequency of hypophosphatemia (<0.65 mmol/L]). The results showed a faster and greater early response with iron isomaltoside for Hb, SF and TSAT compared to iron sucrose. There was no staTsTcally significant difference in the frequency of serious or severe hypersensiTvity reacTons between the treatment groups. The incidence of composite cardiovascular AEs was staTsTcally significantly lower in the isomaltoside group compared to the sucrose group (4.1% vs 6.9%; p=0.25).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A prospecTve case control study from rural India comparing oral (ferrous sulfate) vs IV iron (iron sucrose found that serum iron, ferriTn and TSAT increased in the IV iron compared to the oral group (21) . There were significant increases in Hb in all stages of kidney disease with IV iron in contrast to not being achieved with oral iron (21) .
texts.text: A prospecTve case control study from rural India comparing oral (ferrous sulfate) vs IV iron (iron sucrose found that serum iron, ferriTn and TSAT increased in the IV iron compared to the oral group (21) . There were significant increases in Hb in all stages of kidney disease with IV iron in contrast to not being achieved with oral iron (21) .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.4 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron Therapy - Upper Limit for Iron Therapy:
texts.text: Guideline 2.4 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron Therapy - Upper Limit for Iron Therapy:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For people not on HD, we recommend that serum ferriTn should not exceed 600 mcg/L in those treated with iron. To achieve this, iron management should be reviewed when ferriTn is >500 mcg/L, recognising that a level of >800 mcg/L may reflect iron toxicity. (1B)
texts.text: For people not on HD, we recommend that serum ferriTn should not exceed 600 mcg/L in those treated with iron. To achieve this, iron management should be reviewed when ferriTn is >500 mcg/L, recognising that a level of >800 mcg/L may reflect iron toxicity. (1B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.4.1
texts.text: Guideline 2.4.1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For people receiving HD, we recommend that proacTve high-dose IV iron, iniTally 600 mg in divided doses in the first month and then 400 mg every month (or equivalent) should be given unless ferriTn >700 mcg/L or TSAT >40%. (1A)
texts.text: For people receiving HD, we recommend that proacTve high-dose IV iron, iniTally 600 mg in divided doses in the first month and then 400 mg every month (or equivalent) should be given unless ferriTn >700 mcg/L or TSAT >40%. (1A)

texts.orig: Guideline 2.4.2
texts.text: Guideline 2.4.2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest a tailored approach by clinicians to assess the risk and benefit of starTng a proacTve IV iron protocol in people who have been established on HD for >12 months. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest a tailored approach by clinicians to assess the risk and benefit of starTng a proacTve IV iron protocol in people who have been established on HD for >12 months. (2B)

texts.orig: Rationale
texts.text: Rationale

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Iron overload is defined as increased total body iron content with the possible risk of organ dysfuncTon (1) . There is no clinically available method that accurately determines total body iron content. An elevated serum ferriTn does not always correlate with elevaTons in liver iron content (2,
texts.text: Iron overload is defined as increased total body iron content with the possible risk of organ dysfuncTon (1) . There is no clinically available method that accurately determines total body iron content. An elevated serum ferriTn does not always correlate with elevaTons in liver iron content (2,

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: 3) . MagneTc resonance imaging provides a reliable assessment of Tssue iron content in people on HD regularly treated with parenteral iron (4) . However, the clinical relevance of increased liver iron remains unclear.
texts.text: 3) . MagneTc resonance imaging provides a reliable assessment of Tssue iron content in people on HD regularly treated with parenteral iron (4) . However, the clinical relevance of increased liver iron remains unclear.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Elevated serum ferriTn together with elevated serum transferrin saturaTon remain the most clinically accurate parameter of iron overload in people with CKD.
texts.text: Elevated serum ferriTn together with elevated serum transferrin saturaTon remain the most clinically accurate parameter of iron overload in people with CKD.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: DisconTnuaTon of adequate maintenance IV iron when an individual's ferriTn is >500 mcg/L produces a populaTon mean that straddles the 600 mcg/L ceiling (5) . Ongoing iron therapy with ferriTn >500 mcg/L results in a higher median ferriTn outcome (6) .
texts.text: DisconTnuaTon of adequate maintenance IV iron when an individual's ferriTn is >500 mcg/L produces a populaTon mean that straddles the 600 mcg/L ceiling (5) . Ongoing iron therapy with ferriTn >500 mcg/L results in a higher median ferriTn outcome (6) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: InterpretaTon of iron status results and deciding on the need for further iron therapy should include a concomitant assessment of changes in Hb level and ESA or HIF-PHI dose over Tme. Examples:
texts.text: InterpretaTon of iron status results and deciding on the need for further iron therapy should include a concomitant assessment of changes in Hb level and ESA or HIF-PHI dose over Tme. Examples:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · A dropping ferriTn as well as decreasing Hb levels signifies blood loss e.g., on HD or bowel related anaemia: iron therapy is indicated; further invesTgaTon may be required depending on the clinical scenario.

texts.orig: · A decreasing ferriTn level aVer iniTaTon of ESA therapy, with a concomitant rise in Hb level indicates a response to ESA with a shiV of iron from stores to bone marrow: further iron therapy is guided by target ferriTn level.
texts.text: A decreasing ferriTn level aVer iniTaTon of ESA therapy, with a concomitant rise in Hb level indicates a response to ESA with a shiV of iron from stores to bone marrow: further iron therapy is guided by target ferriTn level.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · A rising ferriTn level and a drop in TSAT suggest an inflammatory condiTon: a source of inflammaTon may be sought such as sepsis, vascular access, surgery, recent hospitalisaTon: further iron therapy depends on target ferriTn level and clinical scenario.
texts.text: A rising ferriTn level and a drop in TSAT suggest an inflammatory condiTon: a source of inflammaTon may be sought such as sepsis, vascular access, surgery, recent hospitalisaTon: further iron therapy depends on target ferriTn level and clinical scenario.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Ongoing high requirements for IV iron to maintain a given ferriTn level also point to ongoing blood loss which may require further invesTgaTon.
texts.text: Ongoing high requirements for IV iron to maintain a given ferriTn level also point to ongoing blood loss which may require further invesTgaTon.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The finding of a TSAT <20% coupled with a ferriTn >500 mcg/L poses a parTcularly difficult problem for clinicians. This situaTon may be caused by iron test variability (7) , inflammaTon, or reTculoendothelial iron blockade. Evidence on the risks and benefits of IV iron therapy in this populaTon is not well established. The effect of iron therapy in this group was assessed in the DRIVE trial (8) , which evaluated the efficacy of IV ferric gluconate in 134 parTcipants with high ferriTn (500-1200 mcg/L) and low TSAT levels (≤ 25%) who were anaemic despite a high rHuEPO dose (≥225 IU/kg/week or ≥22500 IU/week). AVer 6 weeks the parTcipants receiving ferric gluconate (125 mg IV at eight consecuTve HD sessions) showed a significant increase in Hb in comparison with controls. However, the study has a number of limitaTons because, given the small sample size and short follow-up, it provides no informaTon about safety and iron overload.
texts.text: The finding of a TSAT <20% coupled with a ferriTn >500 mcg/L poses a parTcularly difficult problem for clinicians. This situaTon may be caused by iron test variability (7) , inflammaTon, or reTculoendothelial iron blockade. Evidence on the risks and benefits of IV iron therapy in this populaTon is not well established. The effect of iron therapy in this group was assessed in the DRIVE trial (8) , which evaluated the efficacy of IV ferric gluconate in 134 parTcipants with high ferriTn (500-1200 mcg/L) and low TSAT levels (≤ 25%) who were anaemic despite a high rHuEPO dose (≥225 IU/kg/week or ≥22500 IU/week). AVer 6 weeks the parTcipants receiving ferric gluconate (125 mg IV at eight consecuTve HD sessions) showed a significant increase in Hb in comparison with controls. However, the study has a number of limitaTons because, given the small sample size and short follow-up, it provides no informaTon about safety and iron overload.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The PIVOTAL trial compared a high-dose, proacTve IV iron sucrose regimen to a low-dose, reacTve IV iron sucrose regimen in 2141 adult people in their first year of HD receiving an ESA (9) . The high-dose group received 600 mg of IV iron iniTally in the first month and then 400 mg IV iron sucrose proacTvely every month unless serum ferriTn was >700 μg/L or transferrin saturaTon (TSAT) ≥40%. The low-dose group received 0 to 400 mg monthly, with a serum ferriTn of <200 μg/L or a TSAT of <20% being a trigger for iron administraTon. The primary end point was the composite of nonfatal myocardial infarcTon, nonfatal stroke, hospitalisaTon for heart failure, or death, assessed in a Tmeto-first-event analysis. Secondary end points included death, hospitalisaTon for any cause, infecTon rate and ESA dose. The median follow-up period was 2.1 years. The high-dose group received a median monthly iron dose of 264 mg, compared with 145 mg in the low-dose group. The median monthly dose of an ESA was 29,757 IU in the high-dose group and 38,805 IU in the low-dose group. Hence the trial found that paTents in the proacTve arm who achieved ferriTn levels ~600 mcg/L required lower ESA doses overall than those paTents who were in the reacTve arm where ferriTn levels were maintained ~ 200 mcg/L.  A total of 320 (29.3%) in the high-dose group had a primary end-point event, compared with 338 (32.3%) in the low-dose group (HR, 0.85; 95% CI, 0.73 to 1.00; P<0.001 for noninferiority; P=0.04 for superiority). There was no significant difference in infecTon rates or hospitalisaTon for any cause between the two groups. The authors concluded that a highdose IV iron regimen administered proacTvely was superior to a low-dose regimen administered reacTvely; resulTng in a lower risk of death or major adverse cardiovascular events and requiring lower doses of ESA and a lower incidence of blood transfusions (9) .
texts.text: The PIVOTAL trial compared a high-dose, proacTve IV iron sucrose regimen to a low-dose, reacTve IV iron sucrose regimen in 2141 adult people in their first year of HD receiving an ESA (9) . The high-dose group received 600 mg of IV iron iniTally in the first month and then 400 mg IV iron sucrose proacTvely every month unless serum ferriTn was >700 μg/L or transferrin saturaTon (TSAT) ≥40%. The low-dose group received 0 to 400 mg monthly, with a serum ferriTn of <200 μg/L or a TSAT of <20% being a trigger for iron administraTon. The primary end point was the composite of nonfatal myocardial infarcTon, nonfatal stroke, hospitalisaTon for heart failure, or death, assessed in a Tmeto-first-event analysis. Secondary end points included death, hospitalisaTon for any cause, infecTon rate and ESA dose. The median follow-up period was 2.1 years. The high-dose group received a median monthly iron dose of 264 mg, compared with 145 mg in the low-dose group. The median monthly dose of an ESA was 29,757 IU in the high-dose group and 38,805 IU in the low-dose group. Hence the trial found that paTents in the proacTve arm who achieved ferriTn levels ~600 mcg/L required lower ESA doses overall than those paTents who were in the reacTve arm where ferriTn levels were maintained ~ 200 mcg/L.  A total of 320 (29.3%) in the high-dose group had a primary end-point event, compared with 338 (32.3%) in the low-dose group (HR, 0.85; 95% CI, 0.73 to 1.00; P<0.001 for noninferiority; P=0.04 for superiority). There was no significant difference in infecTon rates or hospitalisaTon for any cause between the two groups. The authors concluded that a highdose IV iron regimen administered proacTvely was superior to a low-dose regimen administered reacTvely; resulTng in a lower risk of death or major adverse cardiovascular events and requiring lower doses of ESA and a lower incidence of blood transfusions (9) .

We recommend an individualised approach by clinicians to assess the risk and benefit of starTng a proacTve iv iron protocol in people who have been established on HD for >12 months but it seems likely to have similar benefits.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The FIND-CKD study assessed the 12-month efficacy and safety of IV ferric carboxymaltose (FCM) compared with oral iron in ND-CKD parTcipants with anaemia and iron deficiency. Two different target ferriTn levels were studied: higher (400-600 mcg/L) or lower (100-200 mcg/L). This RCT found that IV FCM targeTng a higher ferriTn is more effecTve than oral iron in delaying or reducing the ESA requirement and the occurrence of 2 consecuTve Hb levels <10 g/dL (10) .

Two different target ferriTn levels were studied: higher (400-600 mcg/L) or lower (100-200 mcg/L). This RCT found that IV FCM targeTng a higher ferriTn is more effecTve than oral iron in delaying or reducing the ESA requirement and the occurrence of 2 consecuTve Hb levels <10 g/dL (10) .

texts.orig: Iron Deficiency and no Anaemia
texts.text: Iron Deficiency and no Anaemia

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is not known whether treatment of people with CKD and Hb values >120 g/L in the presence of iron deficiency is beneficial. The Iron and Heart mulTcentre double-blinded randomised controlled clinical trial examined the effects of a single dose of intravenous iron vs. placebo on funcTonal capacity using the 6-minute walk test, in non-anaemic iron deficient people (serum ferriTn <100 μg/L and/or TSAT<20%) with NDD-CKD stage 3b-5. 54 individuals were randomised to receive ferric derisomaltose (n = 26) or placebo (n = 28) (1-3) . Secondary analysis showed no impact on markers of oxidaTve stress, inflammaTon, and endothelial funcTon. There was a modest but staTsTcally significant rise in E-selecTn in the IV iron group at 1-month and 3-month follow-up (p = 0.030 and p = 0.002 respecTvely). These results suggest ferric derisomaltose administraTon in people with NDDCKD iron deficient does not induce prolonged oxidaTve stress or inflammaTon. These preliminary secondary analyses suggest larger trials are required to quanTfy the benefit of IV iron administraTon in this group.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The FAIR-HF trial enrolled 459 parTcipants with NYHA class II or III, iron deficiency (ferriTn <100 µg/l or between 100 and 299 µ g/l if TSATs <20%), Hb 95 - 135 g/L (4) . ParTcipants were randomised to receive 200 mg of IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) or saline. The primary end points were NYHA funcTonal class and self-reported PaTent Global Assessment at 24 weeks. The distance walked in 6 minutes was a secondary end point. ParTcipants receiving ferric carboxymaltose, 50% reported being much or moderately improved on the PaTent Global Assessment as compared to 28% of parTcipants receiving placebo (OR, 2.51; 95% CI: 1.75 to 3.61). In the ferric carboxymaltose group, 47% had a NYHA funcTonal class I or II at week 24, as compared with 30% of parTcipants assigned placebo (OR for improvement by one class, 2.40; 95% CI: 1.55 to 3.71). Significant improvements were seen in distance on 6-minute walk test. In a sub-group analysis, the benefits of ferric carboxymaltose were seen in people with eGFR both above and less than 60 ml/min/1.73m 2 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CONFIRM-HF trial enrolled 304 symptomaTc parTcipants with heart failure with leV ventricular ejecTon fracTon < or = 45%, elevated natriureTc pepTdes, and iron deficiency (ferriTn <100 ng/mL or 100-300 ng/mL if TSATS <20%) (5) . ParTcipants were randomised to either IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose, given as 500 or 1000 mg) or placebo. The primary end point was 6-min-walk-test (6MWT) distance from baseline to week 24. Treatment with FCM significantly prolonged 6MWT at week 24 (difference FCM vs. placebo: 33 ± 11 m, p = 0.002). The finding was consistent across all subgroups including those with eGFR < 60 mL/min/1.73m 2 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Iron and Heart trial and the Iron and Muscle trial both randomised parTcipants to intravenous iron (ferric derisomaltose and ferric carboxymaltose in the two studies respecTvely (2, 7) . Both recruited parTcipants with CKD (eGFR < 60 mL/min/1.73m 2 ) and defined iron deficiency as a ferriTn < 100µg/L and/or TSATs < 20%. Both used 6MWT distance one month as the primary outcome. Iron and Heart recruited 54 parTcipants and the Iron and Muscle trial recruited 75. AdjusTng for baseline 6MWT there was no staTsTcally significant difference in 6MWT distance at 1 month in either study ( p = 0.26 and 0.74 in the two studies respecTvely). A subgroup in the Iron and Muscle trial had mitochondrial funcTon assessed by two methodologies: respirometry and by 31-phosphorus magneTc resonance spectroscopy. There was no difference in mitochondrial funcTon between placebo and iron groups at 1 month ( p = 0.84 and 0.84 for the two methodologies respecTvely).

It should be noted that people with CKD were not specifically included or analysed in the studies examined (8) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two randomised controlled trials explored the use of IV iron in the treatment of restless leg syndrome (RLS) in HD paTents. They both idenTfied a significant but transient improvement in symptoms. However, since IV iron is now an established treatment in haemodialysis they have limited impact (9, 10) .

They both idenTfied a significant but transient improvement in symptoms. However, since IV iron is now an established treatment in haemodialysis they have limited impact (9, 10) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: They were both included in a systemaTc review which found that treatment of RLS with iron was associated with an increased rate of adverse events RR 2.04 (95% CI: 1.46-2.85), however these were not severe and not associated with treatment disconTnuaTon. Overall, iron supplementaTon was associated with improvement of RLS (11) .

Some have suggested consideraTon of treatment or people with 'profound iron deficiency defined as a serum ferriTn of <30 mcg/L and TSAT <16% but data is lacking to confirm this.

texts.text: Rationale

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All forms of IV iron may be associated with acute adverse events (AEs). Immune mechanisms (including IgE-mediated responses or complement acTvaTon-related pseudo-allergy) may have a role in some cases (1) .  Anaphylactoid reacTons appear to occur more frequently with high molecular weight iron dextran (2) . The rate of life-threatening reacTons to iron dextran administraTon is 0.6% to 0.7% (3,4) . Labile or free iron reacTons occur more frequently with non-dextran forms of iron (4) . These reacTons, also known as Fishbane reacTons, are infusion rate related and are characterised by facial flushing, back pain and chest Tghtness someTmes accompanied with dyspnoea. They can oVen be mistaken for anaphylaxis, however symptoms oVen disappear following cessaTon of the infusion. PaTents should have their vital signs monitored following cessaTon of the infusion and if symptoms have disappeared, can conTnue the therapy at a lower infusion rate. This does not exclude the paTent form receiving that form of IV iron in the future, however a slower rate of infusion would be required for subsequent treatment. (1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In one study, a total of 2534 people receiving haemodialysis were directly observed aVer doubleblind exposure to intravenous sodium ferric gluconate (SFGC) or placebo. One parTcipant in each of the SFGC and placebo groups experienced anaphylactoid reacTons. AddiTonal cases with characterisTcs possibly consistent with anaphylaxis occurred in 0.4% of intravenous SFGC-treated parTcipants and 0.1% of placebo-treated parTcipants. The results suggest that there is a relaTvely low rate of anaphylaxis with non-dextran irons and that the reacTons are generally easily managed (5, 6) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A retrospecTve cohort study compared anaphylaxis rates between five IV iron preparaTons: iron dextran, ferumoxytol, ferric gluconate, iron sucrose and ferric carboxymaltose. The adjusted IRs for anaphylaxis per 10 000 first administraTons were 9.8 cases (95% CI: 6.2 to 15.3 cases) for iron dextran, 4.0 cases (CI: 2.5 to 6.6 cases) for ferumoxytol, 1.5 cases (CI: 0.3 to 6.6 cases) for ferric gluconate, 1.2 cases (CI: 0.6 to 2.5 cases) for iron sucrose, and 0.8 cases (CI: 0.3 to 2.6 cases) for ferric carboxymaltose (7) .

texts.orig: In the IRONMAN trial, 1127 people with heart failure with HFrEF and iron deficiency were recruited to either receive IV ferric derisomaltose (n=559) or usual care (n=568) (8) . There were no differences between the two groups and the rate of serious adverse events. Only one person in the study had an infusion related reacTon, reported to have experienced vomiTng, back pain and dizziness aVer commencing infusion, but recovered fully aVer overnight observaTon (8) .

texts.orig: The MHRA has issued an updated guidance on the use of parenteral iron. This was in response to concerns raised as a result of serious and rarely fatal hypersensiTvity reacTon, parTcularly in pregnant women. These reacTons can occur even when a previous administraTon has been tolerated (including a negaTve test dose). The risk of hypersensiTvity is increased in people with: known allergies (including drug allergies); immune or inflammatory condiTons (e.g., systemic lupus erythematous, rheumatoid arthriTs); or those with a history of severe asthma, eczema, or other atopic allergy. As a result, the MHRA updated guidelines recommend that (9) :

texts.orig: · IV iron should be administered in strict accordance with the posology and method of administraTon described in the product informaTon for each individual product (note that advice varies between products).

texts.label: list_item

texts.text: Recipients should be closely monitored for signs of hypersensiTvity during and for at least 30 minutes aVer every administraTon of an IV iron product.

texts.orig: Rationale
texts.text: Rationale

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Parenteral iron administraTon to people receiving haemodialysis has been shown to result in a reducTon of circulaTng TNFα levels (1) . In addiTon, chronic iron loading has been associated with an impaired immune response of circulaTng monocytes to ex vivo sTmulaTon with lipo-poly saccharide (LPS) (2) . Excess iron inhibits anT-microbial effector pathways of macrophages (3, 4) . This is exerted via blockade of LPS and interferon-gamma (IFNJ) inducible immune pathways, while producTon of macrophage de-acTvaTng cytokines such as interleukin10 (IL10) is increased (5, 6) . The effect of iron on immune funcTon could be dependent on the iron preparaTon; one study has shown that iron sucrose had more prominent effects on monocyte differenTaTon than other clinically available compounds (7) .
texts.text: Parenteral iron administraTon to people receiving haemodialysis has been shown to result in a reducTon of circulaTng TNFα levels (1) . In addiTon, chronic iron loading has been associated with an impaired immune response of circulaTng monocytes to ex vivo sTmulaTon with lipo-poly saccharide (LPS) (2) . Excess iron inhibits anT-microbial effector pathways of macrophages (3, 4) . This is exerted via blockade of LPS and interferon-gamma (IFNJ) inducible immune pathways, while producTon of macrophage de-acTvaTng cytokines such as interleukin10 (IL10) is increased (5, 6) . The effect of iron on immune funcTon could be dependent on the iron preparaTon; one study has shown that iron sucrose had more prominent effects on monocyte differenTaTon than other clinically available compounds (7) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ishida and Johansen criTcally reviewed available literature regarding the associaTon between iron and infecTon in people receiving HD (8) . The authors idenTfied studies that evaluated the associaTon between the risk of infecTon, serum ferriTn levels (13 studies) and iron usage (24 studies). Thirteen studies with sample sizes ranging from 61 to 2,662 have examined the link between serum ferriTn and infecTon in haemodialysis paTents. Among the 13 studies, nine studies reported an associaTon, and four studies did not find an associaTon between serum ferriTn and infecTon. Among the studies that idenTfied an associaTon, high serum ferriTn (typically defined as >500 or 1,000 microgram/L) was associated with higher incidence of bacterial infecTon or infecTon-related mortality. The incidence of bacterial infecTon ranged from 0.34 to 0.59 infecTons per paTent-year (in studies evaluaTng the rate of infecTon) and 0.93% to 61.9% (in studies evaluaTng the proporTon with infecTon) in the higher serum ferriTn groups and 0.09 to 0.18 infecTons per paTent-year and 0% to 37% in the lower serum ferriTn groups. The authors concluded that these studies suggest an excess of 16 to 50 infecTons per 100 paTent-years in the higher compared with the lower serum ferriTn groups. In studies that expressed the associaTon between serum ferriTn and bacterial infecTon as raTos, higher serum ferriTn was independently associated with a 1.5 to 3.1-fold higher incidence of bacterial infecTon or infecTon-related mortality. Among the 24 studies that evaluated the relaTonship between iron therapy and infecTon, twenty-two studies were observaTonal with sample sizes ranging from 21 to 309,219 parTcipants. Twelve of these studies found an associaTon between any iron usage, higher dose or frequency of iron usage and infecTon or infecTon-related mortality.

texts.orig: One study compared mortality with different dosing paXerns of IV iron (9) . Based on data from 117,050 people receiving HD, the study evaluated the effect of bolus versus maintenance IV iron dosing during repeated 1- month exposure periods on risks of mortality and infecTon-related hospitalisaTon during the subsequent 3 months. In mulTvariable addiTve risk models, compared to maintenance dosing (median monthly dose 200 mg), bolus dosing (median 700 mg) was associated with an increased risk of infecTon-related hospitalisaTon (risk difference, 25 addiTonal events/1000 paTent-years; 95% CI: 16 to 33), with the risk being largest among paTents with a catheter or history

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A mulTcentre study prospecTvely evaluated the associaTon between serum ferriTn levels and IV iron usage with adverse outcomes and mortality among 1086 Japanese people receiving chronic HD. By using Cox proporTonal hazard models and Tme-dependent variables, there was a significantly higher risk of infecTon with higher (above 100 microgram/L) compared to lower (below 100 ng/dl) serum ferriTn levels, and with high (≥50 mg/week) and even low (<50 mg/week) doses of IV iron compared with no IV iron; they also reported significantly higher risk of death among people with highamplitude ferriTn fluctuaTons (serum ferriTn level consistently above 100 microgram/L or upward trend from below to above 100 microgram/L) compared with those with low ferriTn level (10) .
texts.text: A mulTcentre study prospecTvely evaluated the associaTon between serum ferriTn levels and IV iron usage with adverse outcomes and mortality among 1086 Japanese people receiving chronic HD. By using Cox proporTonal hazard models and Tme-dependent variables, there was a significantly higher risk of infecTon with higher (above 100 microgram/L) compared to lower (below 100 ng/dl) serum ferriTn levels, and with high (≥50 mg/week) and even low (<50 mg/week) doses of IV iron compared with no IV iron; they also reported significantly higher risk of death among people with highamplitude ferriTn fluctuaTons (serum ferriTn level consistently above 100 microgram/L or upward trend from below to above 100 microgram/L) compared with those with low ferriTn level (10) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a study involving 626 people with pre-dialysis CKD. People were treated with intravenous ferric carboxymaltose (with a high and low ferriTn target) or oral iron for 52 weeks. The percentage of deaths, myocardial infarcTons, and infecTons was not significantly different between oral irontreated and IVI-treated parTcipants. However, the study was not powered to evaluate safety of parenteral iron (11) .

texts.orig: Post-hoc analysis studied adverse events between the three arms. The incidence of one or more adverse events was 91.0, 100.0 and 105.0 per 100 paTent-years in the high ferriTn, low ferriTn and oral iron groups, respecTvely. The incidence of adverse events with a suspected relaTon to study drug was 15.9, 17.8 and 36.7 per 100 paTent-years in the three groups; for serious adverse events, the incidence was 28.2, 27.9 and 24.3 per 100 paTent-years. The incidence of cardiac disorders and infecTons was similar between groups (12) .

texts.orig: In a study evaluaTng the safety of parenteral iron therapy in 10,169 people receiving haemodialysis in the United States; aVer adjusTng for 23 demographic and comorbidity characterisTcs among 5833 people included in the mulTvariable analysis; bills for ≤10 vials of iron over 6 months showed no adverse effect on survival when compared with none, but bills for >10 vials showed a staTsTcally significant elevated rate of death (13) . Bills for ≤10 vials of iron over 6 months also showed no significant associaTon with hospitalisaTon (adjusted = 0.92; 95% CI: 0.83 to 1.03; P = 0.15), but bills for >10 vials showed staTsTcally significant elevated risk. More intensive dosing was associated with diminished survival and higher rates of hospitalisaTon, even aVer extensive adjustment for baseline comorbidity (13) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the IRONMAN trial, 1127 people with heart failure and reduced ejecTon fracTon (HFrEF) and iron deficiency were recruited to either receive IV ferric derisomaltose (n=559) or usual care (n=568). It was found that between the two groups, there were a similar rates of infecTons (25% vs 29%. HR 3.12; 95% CI: -8.30 to 2.06. p=0.24). Deaths due to infecTons were also similar in the two groups (6% vs 5%. HR 1.22, 95% CI: 0.74 to 2.02. p=0.43) as were hospitalisaTons due to infecTon (n= 175 vs 213. HR 0.82, 95% CI: 0.62 to 1.08. p=0.16) (19) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The PIVOTAL trial randomised 2141 people receiving HD who were within the first year of receiving haemodialysis to a high dose proacTve iron regimen (400 mg monthly) or a low dose reacTve regimen (0-400 mg monthly) based of TSAT and ferriTn, showing a high dose iron regimen was superior in reducing rates of non-fatal MI, non-fatal stroke, hospitalisaTon due to heart failure or death (20) . Secondary analysis sought to address concerns around increasing infecTon rates in paTents receiving IV iron. Secondary endpoints included infecTon, hospitalisaTon for infecTon and death from infecTon. It was found that there was no significant difference in secondary endpoints in the high dose vs low dose iron groups.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Results for 'all infecTon episodes,' there were 508 first events (46.5%) in the proacTve high-dose group versus 477 first events (45.5%) in the low-dose group (HR, 0.98; 95% CI: 0.87 to 1.11; P=0.80). Results for 'hospitalisaTons for infecTons' were 323 first events (29.6%) in the proacTve high-dose arm versus 307 first events (29.3%) in the reacTve low-dose arm (HR, 0.99; 95% CI: 0.82 to 1.16; P=0.92). Results for 'death from infecTons,' showed 46 events (4.21%) in the proacTve high-dose arm versus 41 first events (3.91%) in the reacTve low-dose arm (HR, 1.04; 95% CI: 0.69 to 1.59; P=0.84). ParTcipants with central venous catheter were more likely to experience an infecTon, however no differences in all three infecTon endpoints (in relaTon to the treatment arms) were seen in those people dialysing via central venous access vs arteriovenous fistula (20, 21) .

texts.orig: Meta-analysis from Hougen et al. also supported PIVOTAL findings by comparing infecTon rates from four RCT studies using high dose IV iron regimens (>400 mg a month) vs low dose IV iron or no iron regimens in people receiving haemodialysis (22) . This study concluded that there were no differences in infecTon rates in the high dose iron group (n=743; relaTve risk, 1.02; 95% confidence interval, 0.74 to 1.41). Eight observaTonal studies using high dose IV iron regiments (>200 mg a month) showed no increase in infecTon risk (n=135,532; pooled hazard raTo, 1.13; 95% confidence interval, 0.99 to 1.28) (22) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Meta-analysis from Shah A et al. reviewed 154 RCTs looking at risk of infecTon with intravenous iron vs oral iron or no iron across various disease states. This study found that intravenous iron was associated with an increased risk of infecTon when compared with oral iron or no iron (RR, 1.17; 95% CI: 1.04-1.31; I2 = 37%; moderate certainty of evidence) most significantly in those with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). Across the trials, there were significant variaTon in in how infecTon was defined or reported, leading to inconsistent reporTng across the studies. There were no differences observed in mortality between the groups (23) .

texts.orig: Rationale
texts.text: Rationale

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The most commonly used oral treatments for iron deficiency anaemia oVen cause gastrointesTnal side effects, leading to disconTnuaTon of treatment. GastrointesTnal side effects most oVen occur due to the conversion of bivalent iron into trivalent iron, along with the formaTon of reacTve oxygen species (ROS). However, due to the nature of vascular access, intravenous iron administraTon is not well-accepted by many people who are not receiving haemodialysis and furthermore can lead to high non bound (labile) iron levels in the circulaTon, resulTng in ROS generaTon.
texts.text: The most commonly used oral treatments for iron deficiency anaemia oVen cause gastrointesTnal side effects, leading to disconTnuaTon of treatment. GastrointesTnal side effects most oVen occur due to the conversion of bivalent iron into trivalent iron, along with the formaTon of reacTve oxygen species (ROS). However, due to the nature of vascular access, intravenous iron administraTon is not well-accepted by many people who are not receiving haemodialysis and furthermore can lead to high non bound (labile) iron levels in the circulaTon, resulTng in ROS generaTon.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To address these concerns, nanosized iron preparaTons have been developed. Reducing the parTcle size of iron compounds increases their surface area, improving solubility in gastric juice and enhancing absorpTon. Novel iron preparaTons like sucrosomial iron, ferric citrate, and ferric maltol offer high bioavailability and good tolerance, making them suitable opTons for anaemia in chronic kidney disease.

texts.orig: Ferric Citrate
texts.text: Ferric Citrate

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ferric citrate is used in people with CKD undergoing dialysis primarily for phosphate binding but is also employed off-label as an iron supplement.
texts.text: Ferric citrate is used in people with CKD undergoing dialysis primarily for phosphate binding but is also employed off-label as an iron supplement.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There have been two meta-analyses of ferric citrate and treatment of anaemia. One analysis of nine studies found that ferric citrate significantly increased haemoglobin levels compared to acTve control drugs (mean difference 0.43 g/dL) or placebo (mean difference 0.39 g/dL) (1) . A second meta-analysis of five studies showed that ferric citrate substanTally increased haemoglobin levels compared to the comparator group (mean difference 0.65 g/dL) along with improvements in other laboratory parameters such as iron, ferriTn, transferrin saturaTon, and bicarbonate levels (2) .
texts.text: There have been two meta-analyses of ferric citrate and treatment of anaemia. One analysis of nine studies found that ferric citrate significantly increased haemoglobin levels compared to acTve control drugs (mean difference 0.43 g/dL) or placebo (mean difference 0.39 g/dL) (1) . A second meta-analysis of five studies showed that ferric citrate substanTally increased haemoglobin levels compared to the comparator group (mean difference 0.65 g/dL) along with improvements in other laboratory parameters such as iron, ferriTn, transferrin saturaTon, and bicarbonate levels (2) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a sequenTal, randomised trial, subjects on ferric citrate achieved higher mean iron parameters (ferriTn = 899±488 ng/ml; transferrin saturaTon = 39%±17%) compared to subjects on acTve control (ferriTn = 628±367 ng/ml; transferrin saturaTon = 30%±12%). Haemoglobin levels were also staTsTcally higher on ferric citrate (3) .

Haemoglobin levels were also staTsTcally higher on ferric citrate (3) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A 12-week, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of ferric citrate of 149 parTcipants with eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73m 2 , Hb, 90-120 g/L; TSAT ≤30%, ferriTn ≤300 ng/mL). Ferric citrate treatment increased mean transferrin saturaTon (TSAT) from 22% ± 7% to 32% ± 14% and increased haemoglobin levels (from 105 ± 8 to 110 ± 10 g/L) compared to placebo (4) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The ASTRIO trial randomised 93 people who were undergoing HD and being treated with non-ironbased phosphate binders and ESA to receive 24 weeks of ferric citrate or to conTnue their non-ironbased phosphate binders. While Hb and Hct were not significantly changed, the ferric citrate group experienced a significant decrease ESA dose requirement compared to the control group (5) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a prospecTve study of 60 parTcipants on HD treated with lanthanum carbonate, parTcipants were randomly assigned to 2 groups: lanthanum carbonate or switch to ferric citrate. The ferric citrate group had significantly increased serum iron, serum ferriTn, transferrin saturaTon, and significantly decreased ESA doses compared to the control group (6) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A randomised double-blind clinical trial in adults with NDD-CKD and iron deficiency anaemia to compare the safety and efficacy of oral ferric citrate (n=117) versus placebo (n=115) was performed (7) . Ferric citrate-treated paTents had a significantly higher proporTon achieving a ≥10 g/L increase in haemoglobin, with a safety profile similar to the placebo group (7). A further analysis of this study found that ferric citrate showed a greater treatment effect on paTents with lower baseline ferriTn levels, resulTng in a greater rate of haemoglobin rise (8) .

texts.orig: In 441 subjects with end-stage kidney failure, ferric citrate (FC) increased ferriTn and transferrin saturaTon levels, reduced intravenous iron use, and lowered ESA requirements (10) . Subjects were randomised to ferric citrate, sevelamer carbonate and/or calcium acetate and followed for 52 weeks. Subjects on FC had increased ferriTn and TSAT levels by week 12 and needed less IV iron and less cumulaTve ESA over 52 weeks (10) .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Ferric Maltol
texts.text: Ferric Maltol

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ferric maltol is a compound composed of ferric iron and maltol, a naturally occurring sugar derivaTve commonly found in various food products. This complex remains Tghtly bound in the intesTnal tract unTl it reaches the point of absorpTon. At this stage, iron exhibits a stronger aXracTon to iron transport receptors on the surface of intesTnal cells, leading to its separaTon from maltol. This unique mechanism ensures that there is no unbound or free iron in the gut, reducing the risk of generaTng harmful hydroxyl radicals and minimizing the potenTal for gastrointesTnal side effects. Iron uptake from ferric maltol is subject to saturaTon and relies on the presence of iron transport receptors, ensuring controlled and efficient absorpTon.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: AEGIS-CKD is a phase 3, double-blind, randomised, placebo-controlled trial, where parTcipants were given oral ferric maltol at 30 mg or placebo twice daily for 16 weeks (2:1 randomisaTon), followed by ferric maltol at 30 mg twice daily for up to 36 weeks (all paTents) (11) . 167 parTcipants were randomised, with 111 receiving ferric maltol and 56 receiving a placebo. At week 16, haemoglobin significantly increased with ferric maltol compared to placebo. FerriTn, transferrin saturaTon, and serum iron levels also increased with ferric maltol but declined with placebo. Haemoglobin levels remained stable up to week 52 in paTents conTnuing ferric maltol, and they increased in paTents switching from placebo to ferric maltol. The most common adverse events were gastrointesTnal, and adverse events led to treatment withdrawal in a small percentage of paTents, with slightly more in the placebo group (11) .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Ferric Pyrophosphate (FPC)
texts.text: Ferric Pyrophosphate (FPC)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: FPC is a complex iron salt which is administered along with dialysate. Two phase 3 trials, CRUISE 1 and 2, involving 599 iron-replete parTcipants receiving chronic haemodialysis having been performed (12) . ParTcipants received dialysate containing FPC-iron or a standard dialysate (placebo) for up to 48 weeks. Results showed that Hb concentraTon was maintained in the FPC group but decreased in the placebo group (0.4 g/dL decrease, p < 0.001). Placebo treatment also resulted in larger decreases in CHr and serum ferriTn compared to FPC treatment. The proporTons of paTents with adverse events were similar in both treatment groups (12) .
texts.text: FPC is a complex iron salt which is administered along with dialysate. Two phase 3 trials, CRUISE 1 and 2, involving 599 iron-replete parTcipants receiving chronic haemodialysis having been performed (12) . ParTcipants received dialysate containing FPC-iron or a standard dialysate (placebo) for up to 48 weeks. Results showed that Hb concentraTon was maintained in the FPC group but decreased in the placebo group (0.4 g/dL decrease, p < 0.001). Placebo treatment also resulted in larger decreases in CHr and serum ferriTn compared to FPC treatment. The proporTons of paTents with adverse events were similar in both treatment groups (12) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A Chinese study invesTgated the pharmacokineTcs and safety of FPC in subjects with and without HD. In healthy subjects, the peak serum concentraTon was reached at the end of the infusion, with a Cmax of 33.46 μmol/L at a Tmax of 4.09 hours. In people receiving HD, there were differences in Cmax and Tmax between two doses of FPC. Adverse events were reported in both healthy subjects and people receiving HD, with a limited number of incidences (13) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a randomised trial of 99 parTcipants with CKD and iron deficiency anaemia (Hb ≤120 g/L, ferriTn ≤100 ng/mL, TSAT ≤25%) were divided into two groups: one received oral liposomal iron (30 mg/day) and the other intravenous (IV) iron gluconate (total dose of 1000 mg, infused weekly) for 3 months. The IV iron group showed a faster increase in haemoglobin levels compared to the liposomal iron group during the first month of treatment, but both groups had similar haemoglobin levels by the end of the 3-month treatment period. AVer disconTnuaTon of iron treatment, haemoglobin levels remained stable in the IV group but returned to baseline in the liposomal iron group. The incidence of adverse events was significantly lower in the oral liposomal iron group (14) .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Hypophosphataemia
texts.text: Hypophosphataemia

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hypophosphataemia is a known side effect of intravenous iron preparaTons as listed in the SmPCs of IV iron products. It is a common side effect with ferric carboxymaltose (FCM) (15) . As detailed in their SmPC, symptomaTc hypophosphataemia leading to osteomalacia and fractures requiring clinical intervenTon including surgery has been reported in the post markeTng seLng. PaTents should be asked to seek medical advice if they experience worsening faTgue with myalgias or bone pain. Serum phosphate should be monitored in paTents who receive mulTple administraTons at higher doses or long-term treatment, and those with exisTng risk factors for hypophosphataemia. In case of persisTng hypophosphataemia, treatment with ferric carboxymaltose should be re-evaluated.

In case of persisTng hypophosphataemia, treatment with ferric carboxymaltose should be re-evaluated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Acute features can include muscle pain and weakness as well as faTgue, although oVen can be asymptomaTc (16) . There is evidence that hypophosphataemia, secondary to IV iron therapy can lead to long term issues with bone metabolism, presenTng as osteomalacia and fractures (16) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Wolf et al. made the first link between FCM causing a rise in Fibroblast growth factor 23 (FGF23), s hormone which regulates phosphate and vitamin D, causing a decrease in phosphate levels, calcitriol and calcium, with an increase in PTH levels. This study compared FCM to iron dextran treatment, measuring the difference in FGF23 and phosphate levels in people with iron deficiency, secondary to heavy uterine bleeding. Authors reported a significant rise in iFGF23 following treatment with FCM, but not iron dextran (r = 0.59; p < 0.001), with corresponding decrease in phosphate (r = -0.50; p = 0.002). Differences in vitamin D, calcium and PTH were not significant. Hypophosphataemia was reported to conTnue for up to 80 days (17) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Following on from this study, sub-analysis of the FIRM trial compared FGF23 and phosphate levels in a trial of FCM vs ferumoxytol in which hypophosphataemia was significantly decreased in the FCM group vs ferumoxytol group (<2.0 mg/dl, 50.8% vs. 0.9%; <1.3 mg/dl, 10.0% vs. 0.0%; P < 0.001), conTnuing for up to 5 weeks aVer FCM administraTon in 29.1% of parTcipants. FCM only increased the FGF23 concentraTons, which lead to a significant decrease in calcium (mean difference between iron treatment groups in change from baseline to week 2, 0.37 mg/dl; 95%CI 0.25-0.50; P < 0.001) and vitamin D (mean within-paTent percentage change from baseline to week 2, -60.4 ± 25.9% vs. 2.5 ± 28.0%; P < 0.001), with a rise in PTH levels (18) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Literature review by Schafer B et al. looked at 42 prospecTve clinical trials, which found that people, who have received FCM, are more likely to experience hypophosphataemia than those who have received FDI, with an incidence of 42% vs 4% across 42 clinical trials (95% CI: 36-58% vs. 95% CI: 25%). Significantly decreased levels in serum phosphate were also seen when comparing the two treatments, a decrease in 0.4mmol/L with FCM vs a decrease in 0.06mmol/L with FDI, with hypophosphataemia reportedly persisTng aVer 3 months of treatment with FCM (7) . The only significant predictor of experiencing hypophosphataemia being the severity of anaemia at Tme of treatment, with no significant link seen between IV iron treatment in CKD (18,22) . However, FGF23 has been reported to increase aVer IV iron therapy in people with CKD, with a decrease in acTve vitamin D levels (23) , despite higher baseline levels of FGF23 in paTents with CKD, affecTng bone metabolism long term (24) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Glaspy J et al. published a literature review that included 40 trials reviewing all of the IV iron products on the US market at the Tme. This included iron dextran, ferric gluconate, FCM, ferumoxytol and iron sucrose. This review found that people without CKD and who receive higher doses of iron are more likely to experience hypophosphataemia (25) . As with previous findings, FCM treatment was associated with the highest rate of reported hypophosphataemia (25) .
texts.text: Glaspy J et al. published a literature review that included 40 trials reviewing all of the IV iron products on the US market at the Tme. This included iron dextran, ferric gluconate, FCM, ferumoxytol and iron sucrose. This review found that people without CKD and who receive higher doses of iron are more likely to experience hypophosphataemia (25) . As with previous findings, FCM treatment was associated with the highest rate of reported hypophosphataemia (25) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Kidney transplant recipients can experience hypophosphataemia within the first 3 months post transplantaTon (secondary to phosphaturia), which is associated with high levels of FGF23 and PTH. This normalises in the recovery period and increasing again with declining graV funcTon, leading to adverse effects on the cardiovascular system, graV funcTon and bone metabolism (26) . 23 kidney transplant recipients received FCM, showing an average decrease in phosphate levels of 0.27 mmol/l (p = 0.003). 56.5% of parTcipants experienced hypophosphataemia, with severe Hypophosphataemia (<0.5 mmol/L) seen in 34.8% of people. Hypophosphataemia persisted in 38.5% of paTents with median normalisaTon of levels at 41 days (27) . This shows that kidney transplant recipients should receive FCM with cauTon and close monitoring (28) .
texts.text: Kidney transplant recipients can experience hypophosphataemia within the first 3 months post transplantaTon (secondary to phosphaturia), which is associated with high levels of FGF23 and PTH. This normalises in the recovery period and increasing again with declining graV funcTon, leading to adverse effects on the cardiovascular system, graV funcTon and bone metabolism (26) . 23 kidney transplant recipients received FCM, showing an average decrease in phosphate levels of 0.27 mmol/l (p = 0.003). 56.5% of parTcipants experienced hypophosphataemia, with severe Hypophosphataemia (<0.5 mmol/L) seen in 34.8% of people. Hypophosphataemia persisted in 38.5% of paTents with median normalisaTon of levels at 41 days (27) . This shows that kidney transplant recipients should receive FCM with cauTon and close monitoring (28) .

texts.orig: therapy is required. Similarly with ongoing iron losses on HD regular monitoring to avoid worsening iron deficiency is required (1, 3) . The safety of persistently very high ferriTn levels remains unknown. In a cohort of 58058 prevalent people receiving haemodialysis in the USA, both all-cause and cardiovascular mortality had increasing rates across increasing ferriTn levels, whereas the opposite (inverse) associaTon was observed for TSAT increments. Serum ferriTn levels between 200 and 1200 microgram/L and iron saturaTon raTo between 30 and 50% were associated with the lowest allcause and cardiovascular death risks. However, associaTon studies are biased by the fact that serum ferriTn is also a marker of inflammaTon. In unadjusted, Tme-varying model, serum ferriTn >800 microgram/L during each quarter was associated with increased death rate (4) . Significant iron overload in the liver and spleen (assessed through T 2 magneTc resonance) has been described in 19 of 21 people receiving HD with serum ferriTn >1000 microgram/L and severe comorbidiTes who were treated with IV iron (5) . Similarly, Rostoker et al (6) . prospecTvely studied a cohort of 119 fit people receiving HD who were receiving iron and ESA therapy and measured their liver iron content by means of T 1 and T 2 magneTc resonance. Mild to severe hepaTc iron overload was observed in 84% of the people, 36% of whom had severe iron overload approaching that found in haemocromatosis (7) .

texts.text: Guideline 3.1- Inves9ga9ons Before Ini9a9ng ESA Therapy:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that all correctable causes of anaemia should be ruled out before considering treatment with ESAs. (1B)
texts.text: We recommend that all correctable causes of anaemia should be ruled out before considering treatment with ESAs. (1B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Rationale
texts.text: Rationale

texts.label: text
texts.orig: InvesTgaTons for iron deficiency, folate and B12 deficiency, hyperparathyroidism, inflammatory states as well as gastro-intesTnal blood losses should be invesTgated and addressed before iniTaTng ESA therapy. By addressing any underlying causes and correcTng any deficiencies, ESA use may be avoided. (1) CorrecTng any deficiencies, including iron deficiency, before starTng ESA treatment will allow for opTmal ESA responses, minimising the dose required to maintain target haemoglobin levels. (2)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 3.2 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron Therapy - Ini9a9on of ESA and Iron Status:
texts.text: Guideline 3.2 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron Therapy - Ini9a9on of ESA and Iron Status:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that ESA therapy should NOT be iniTated in the presence of absolute iron deficiency, (ferriTn <100 mcg/L in people with non-dialysis dependent CKD (NDD-CKD) and <200 mcg/L in people who are dialysis dependent) unTl this is corrected and it is determined that anaemia persists in conjuncTon to a shared decision of the advantages and risks of ESA therapy. In people with funcTonal iron deficiency iron supplements should be given prior to or when iniTaTng ESA therapy. (1B)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Iron is required for producTon of new red cells. Iron must be supplied to the erythropoieTc Tssue at an adequate rate, parTcularly if sTmulated by ESA therapy. If iron stores are low ESAs can sTll be used if anaemia of CKD is a likely contributor, as long as iron is made directly available to the erythropoieTc Tssues coincident with the iniTaTon of ESA therapy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The PIVOTAL trial has shown that in people receiving haemodialysis on a high-dose proacTve iron regimen (400 mg a month aVer an iniTal dose of 600 mg in the first month) require smaller ESA doses to maintain desirable Hb level between 100-120 g/L, when compared to those paTents on low dose iron regimen (0-400 mg monthly when required to maintain ferriTn >200 μg/L and a transferrin saturaTon >20%). The median monthly dose of erythropoiesis-sTmulaTng agent was 19.4% lower in people receiving the high-dose regimen (29,757 IU per month; interquarTle range, 18,673 to 48,833) than in paTents receiving the low-dose regimen (38,805 IU per month; interquarTle range, 24,377 to 60,620) (median difference, -7539 IU per month; 95% CI, -9485 to -5582). The people in the high dose arm experienced less cardiovascular events, which may be contributed to lower ESA doses use due to pro-acTve iron therapy (2) .
texts.text: The PIVOTAL trial has shown that in people receiving haemodialysis on a high-dose proacTve iron regimen (400 mg a month aVer an iniTal dose of 600 mg in the first month) require smaller ESA doses to maintain desirable Hb level between 100-120 g/L, when compared to those paTents on low dose iron regimen (0-400 mg monthly when required to maintain ferriTn >200 μg/L and a transferrin saturaTon >20%). The median monthly dose of erythropoiesis-sTmulaTng agent was 19.4% lower in people receiving the high-dose regimen (29,757 IU per month; interquarTle range, 18,673 to 48,833) than in paTents receiving the low-dose regimen (38,805 IU per month; interquarTle range, 24,377 to 60,620) (median difference, -7539 IU per month; 95% CI, -9485 to -5582). The people in the high dose arm experienced less cardiovascular events, which may be contributed to lower ESA doses use due to pro-acTve iron therapy (2) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several smaller prospecTve trials have also been carried out to confirm what was found in the PIVOTAL trial. One study compared the ESA requirements in Iranian people receiving haemodialysis receiving regular low dose iron (100 mg/month, n=30) and high dose iron (400 mg/month, n=30) regimens. It was found that the ESA dosage to maintain hemoglobin level between 100 and 120 g/L was significantly decreased in the high dose iron arm (52,129.03 ± 23,810 vs. 45,760 ± 20,978.71, P ≤ 0.02) (3) .
texts.text: Several smaller prospecTve trials have also been carried out to confirm what was found in the PIVOTAL trial. One study compared the ESA requirements in Iranian people receiving haemodialysis receiving regular low dose iron (100 mg/month, n=30) and high dose iron (400 mg/month, n=30) regimens. It was found that the ESA dosage to maintain hemoglobin level between 100 and 120 g/L was significantly decreased in the high dose iron arm (52,129.03 ± 23,810 vs. 45,760 ± 20,978.71, P ≤ 0.02) (3) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another study also compared ESA requirements in Thai people receiving haemodialysis prescribed regular low dose iron (100 mg/month, n=40) and regular high dose iron (200 mg/month, n=39). Similarly, this showed reduced ESA requirements, to maintain a desirable Hb level of 100-120 g/L, in those paTents on the high dose iron regiment. The mean monthly ESA dose at month 12, was 35,706 ± 21,637 IU in the 100-mg IV iron group compared with 26,382 ± 14,983 IU in the 200-mg IV iron group (P = 0.03). No significance was seen between the two groups when looking at the death rate, cardiovascular events, blood transfusions or EQ-5D quality of life measurements (4) .
texts.text: Another study also compared ESA requirements in Thai people receiving haemodialysis prescribed regular low dose iron (100 mg/month, n=40) and regular high dose iron (200 mg/month, n=39). Similarly, this showed reduced ESA requirements, to maintain a desirable Hb level of 100-120 g/L, in those paTents on the high dose iron regiment. The mean monthly ESA dose at month 12, was 35,706 ± 21,637 IU in the 100-mg IV iron group compared with 26,382 ± 14,983 IU in the 200-mg IV iron group (P = 0.03). No significance was seen between the two groups when looking at the death rate, cardiovascular events, blood transfusions or EQ-5D quality of life measurements (4) .

texts.text: Guideline 3.3 - Treatment of Anaemia - Erythropoiesis S9mula9ng Agents:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that treatment with Erythropoiesis STmulaTng Agents (ESAs) should be offered to people with anaemia of CKD, who are likely to benefit in terms of quality of life and physical funcTon and to avoid blood transfusion; especially in people considered suitable for transplantaTon. (1B)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Treatment of anaemia in CKD with ESA can be expensive (1) , takes Tme to work and carries a small but significant risk to the paTent. It is therefore reasonable, as with any therapy, to treat only those who are expected to benefit in the Tme frame that therapy is being considered. For example, people with severe sepsis/inflammaTon/acute bleeding are unlikely to respond.
texts.text: Treatment of anaemia in CKD with ESA can be expensive (1) , takes Tme to work and carries a small but significant risk to the paTent. It is therefore reasonable, as with any therapy, to treat only those who are expected to benefit in the Tme frame that therapy is being considered. For example, people with severe sepsis/inflammaTon/acute bleeding are unlikely to respond.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: People with a very short life expectancy (days or weeks) are not likely to survive long enough for therapy to provide benefit in terms of an increase in Hb. The clinician and paTent should agree on a therapeuTc plan and, at an appropriate Tme, review whether therapy is providing enough benefit to conTnue treatment.

texts.orig: Rationale for Guidelines 3.4 and 3.5
texts.text: Rationale for Guidelines 3.4 and 3.5

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In determining target Hb guidelines it is important to assess potenTal benefits (in terms of possible improved survival, improvement in health-related quality of life (HRQoL) and avoidance of transfusion requirement and hospitalisaTon) vs. potenTal harms (increased mortality, increased risk of vascular events).
texts.text: In determining target Hb guidelines it is important to assess potenTal benefits (in terms of possible improved survival, improvement in health-related quality of life (HRQoL) and avoidance of transfusion requirement and hospitalisaTon) vs. potenTal harms (increased mortality, increased risk of vascular events).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although several studies have shown that higher Hb targets could be associated with improvements in both physical and mental health domains (1) , the HRQoL benefits of higher Hb targets diminish over Tme (1) . In addiTon, there is no apparent Hb threshold above which there is definiTvely a quality-oflife improvement in the higher Hb treatment arms.
texts.text: Although several studies have shown that higher Hb targets could be associated with improvements in both physical and mental health domains (1) , the HRQoL benefits of higher Hb targets diminish over Tme (1) . In addiTon, there is no apparent Hb threshold above which there is definiTvely a quality-oflife improvement in the higher Hb treatment arms.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Besarab et al (2) reported a study of normalisaTon of haemoglobin in 1233 prevalent people with receiving HD with high cardiovascular risk on haemodialysis. NormalisaTon of haemoglobin showed no benefit in risk reducTon but did show an improvement in quality of life. The treatment arm showed a trend towards increased risk of death or cardiac event (183 deaths and 19 myocardial infarcts, producing 202 primary events, compared to 164 events (150 deaths, 14 myocardial infarcts) and vascular access (39% versus 29%) and the trial was terminated before compleTon on the grounds that the study was unlikely to show benefit from normalisaTon.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two trials evaluated the effect of ESA on people not yet receiving dialysis - CHOIR (3) and CREATE (4) . outcome of the CHOIR trial showed no benefit of higher Hb outcome in people with NDD-CKD (eGFR 15-50ml/min/1.73m 2 ) randomised to Hb of 113 g/L vs. 135 g/L. Higher outcome target Hb had an increased risk (using composite endpoints of death, myocardial infarcTon, or hospitalisaTon for congesTve cardiac failure) and no incremental improvement in quality of life (3) . The limitaTon of this trial is that, compared with the group assigned to the lower Hb treatment target, the higher Hb target group showed at baseline a staTsTcally greater proporTon of paTents with a history of hypertension and coronary artery bypass graV. A report posted by the trial sponsor (5) indicates that paTents assigned to the higher Hb treatment arm also had a significantly greater severity of congesTve heart failure (CHF) at baseline. The results of a mulTvariate analysis, included in this report, indicate that aVer adjustment for baseline condiTons (CHF by NaTonal Health and NutriTon ExaminaTon Survey CHF score, atrial fibrillaTon/fluXer, serum albumin level, reTculocyte count, and age), the relaTonship between treatment assignment and primary composite outcome events is no longer staTsTcally significant (HR, 1.24; 95% confidence interval [CI], 0.95 to 1.62; p = 0.11 compared with the unadjusted HR of 1.34; 95% CI: 1.03 to 1.74; p = 0.03) (6). A secondary analysis of the CHOIR trial suggested that higher doses of epoeTn α, rather than target Hb per se, were associated with an increased risk of death, myocardial infarcTon, congesTve heart failure or stroke compared with lower epoeTn doses, and with poorer outcomes (6) . Another secondary analysis of the CHOIR trial found that, among paTents with diabetes mellitus, the percentage of people reaching the primary end point of death, myocardial infarcTon, congesTve heart failure or stroke within 3 years was similar in the high and low haemoglobin arms of the trial (24.8% versus 24.7%, respecTvely; p = 0.249). By contrast, among paTents without diabetes mellitus at baseline, 36.4% of parTcipants randomised to the higher Hb target had reached the primary end point aVer 3 years compared with 24% of those randomised to the lower haemoglobin target (HR 1.70; 95% CI: 1.03-2.81; p= 0.04). Individuals without diabetes mellitus randomised to the higher haemoglobin target had a significantly greater risk of reaching the primary end point aVer 3 years than individuals with diabetes mellitus randomised to the lower haemoglobin target (7) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CREATE (4) trial reported that early correcTon of anaemia to normal Hb (130-150 g/L vs. 105-115 g/L) did not reduce risk of cardiovascular events. Indeed, the hazards raTo for primary endpoints of death from any cause or death from cardiovascular disease consistently (but not significantly) favoured the lower haemoglobin target group. The trend to increase in events appeared to occur aVer iniTaTon of dialysis but there was no difference in endpoints aVer censoring of data from paTents who started dialysis. Quality of life was significantly beXer in the higher Hb outcome group. Although GFR was not significantly different between the two groups, more people started kidney replacement therapy earlier in the higher Hb outcome group (p =0.03) with the difference apparent from 18 months. An important limitaTon of this trial is that the event rate was much lower than predicted; thus, the power to detect a difference in event rates was decreased (4) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Other important limitaTon (s) of these trials is that important subgroups of parTcipants enrolled in large trials, such as young adults, people returning to dialysis aVer failed kidney transplant, or people with chronic lung disease were not idenTfied or assessed in any of these trials.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CKD, including, CHOIR, CREATE and other studies. Combining mortality outcomes from eight studies involving 3038 subjects with CKD (the CHOIR and CREATE trials contributed most of the weight to the analysis) revealed no difference between the higher and lower Hb target (7) , but combining adverse cardiovascular events from six studies involving 2850 subjects showed an increased risk among the paTents assigned to the higher Hb targets (a RR of 1.24, 95% CI: 1.02-1.51) (8) , although it is worth noTng that the CHOIR and CREATE trials also contributed most of the weight to the analysis. Among dialysis paTents, combining mortality (four studies, 2391 subjects) or cardiovascular outcomes (three studies, 1975 subjects) showed no staTsTcally significant difference between the higher and lower Hb level with The US Normal Haematocrit Trial (2) contribuTng most of the weight to the analysis.
