texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 5.1.2
texts.text: Guideline 5.1.2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest using single unit transfusion, where possible for stable non-bleeding people with CKD who clinically require transfusion. (2C)
texts.text: We suggest using single unit transfusion, where possible for stable non-bleeding people with CKD who clinically require transfusion. (2C)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 5.1.3
texts.text: Guideline 5.1.3

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest that kidney transplant recipients, those on the transplant waiTng list or people on immunosuppressive therapy should receive only hepaTTs E negaTve blood components (all UK blood components are tested), but neither CMV negaTve nor irradiated blood is required. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest that kidney transplant recipients, those on the transplant waiTng list or people on immunosuppressive therapy should receive only hepaTTs E negaTve blood components (all UK blood components are tested), but neither CMV negaTve nor irradiated blood is required. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Rationale for Guideline 5.1
texts.text: Rationale for Guideline 5.1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CKD results in chronic anaemia, the degree of which usually reflects the severity of CKD. As with any chronic anaemia, paTents tend to deal with this by various compensatory mechanisms. Blood transfusion is rarely an acute requirement except in emergencies such as acute blood loss, acute haemolysis or severe sepsis/inflammaTon. Hence the risk benefit raTo of the intervenTon needs to be analysed before prescribing a red blood cell transfusion to treat anaemia in people with chronic kidney disease.
texts.text: CKD results in chronic anaemia, the degree of which usually reflects the severity of CKD. As with any chronic anaemia, paTents tend to deal with this by various compensatory mechanisms. Blood transfusion is rarely an acute requirement except in emergencies such as acute blood loss, acute haemolysis or severe sepsis/inflammaTon. Hence the risk benefit raTo of the intervenTon needs to be analysed before prescribing a red blood cell transfusion to treat anaemia in people with chronic kidney disease.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The potenTal risks associated with blood transfusion include transfusion reacTons, iron overload and transfusion acquired infecTons. In the presence of severe chronic anaemia, transfusion may lead to congesTve cardiac failure, parTcularly in the elderly. A review of transfusion outcome in people with acute coronary artery syndromes revealed a greater mortality rate in transfusion recipients (1) . Another review suggested that treatment of mild to moderate anaemia resulted in increased mortality (2) . Also transplant recipient sensiTsaTon may occur following transfusion resulTng in longer transplant register waiTng Tmes and difficulty in finding a cross match negaTve donor. A study from Ireland looking at causes of sensiTsaTon of potenTal allograV recipients showed that the level of sensiTsaTon increased with the number of units of blood transfused and also demonstrated a direct relaTonship between degree of sensiTsaTon and waiTng Tme for transplantaTon (3) . Blood transfusions can induce anTbodies to histocompaTbility leukocyte anTgens that can reduce the success of kidney transplantaTon; thus transfusions generally should be avoided in paTents awaiTng a kidney transplant (2) .
texts.text: The potenTal risks associated with blood transfusion include transfusion reacTons, iron overload and transfusion acquired infecTons. In the presence of severe chronic anaemia, transfusion may lead to congesTve cardiac failure, parTcularly in the elderly. A review of transfusion outcome in people with acute coronary artery syndromes revealed a greater mortality rate in transfusion recipients (1) . Another review suggested that treatment of mild to moderate anaemia resulted in increased mortality (2) . Also transplant recipient sensiTsaTon may occur following transfusion resulTng in longer transplant register waiTng Tmes and difficulty in finding a cross match negaTve donor. A study from Ireland looking at causes of sensiTsaTon of potenTal allograV recipients showed that the level of sensiTsaTon increased with the number of units of blood transfused and also demonstrated a direct relaTonship between degree of sensiTsaTon and waiTng Tme for transplantaTon (3) . Blood transfusions can induce anTbodies to histocompaTbility leukocyte anTgens that can reduce the success of kidney transplantaTon; thus transfusions generally should be avoided in paTents awaiTng a kidney transplant (2) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The use of ESAs can greatly reduce the need for red blood cell transfusions in people with anaemia of CKD when target Hb concentraTons are achieved and maintained (4-5) . Since the introducTon of ESAs and reducTon in rouTne blood transfusion in people with anaemia and CKD, sensiTsaTon has markedly reduced (6) . With the advent of new immunosuppressant regimens aVer 1995, the use of pre-transplantaTon transfusion have been rendered largely obsolete (7) . The K-DOQI anaemia guideline recommends that no single Hb concentraTon jusTfies or requires transfusion and the target Hb recommended for chronic anaemia management should not serve as a transfusion trigger (8) . NICE agrees that there are clinical reasons to minimise blood transfusion in anaemia of CKD and if blood transfusion is essenTal the relevant haematology guidelines should be followed (e.g. the BriTsh Society for Haematology (BSH) guidelines hXps://b-s-h.org.uk/guidelines/guidelines) (8) . In hospitalised paTents who are haemodynamically stable, the need for transfusion is directed by symptoms and the Hb values. Whilst one study suggested that a value in CKD paTents of <70 g/L or <80 g/L in post-operaTve surgical paTents or pre-exisTng cardiac disease should prompt transfusion , transfusion should be based on paTent assessment and policies set by local transfusion guidelines (9) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: HepaTTs E virus (HEV) is a RNA virus and has 4 genotypes: the one commonly found in the UK is genotype 3. The most common route of infecTon in the UK is from eaTng raw or undercooked meat (parTcularly pork products) and shellfish; however, HEV can be transmiXed via blood transfusion and solid organ transplantaTon. Incidence of HEV in the UK has been increasing considerably since 2011. It was esTmated that as many as 100,000 persons may suffer acute infecTons each year and that less than 1 in 100 will have any illness at all. The majority of people who become infected with HEV have no symptoms and the infecTon clears completely within a couple of months. HEV may pose a risk of harm to immunocompromised paTents who may be unable to clear the infecTon, which may then become persistent, potenTally leading to chronic inflammaTon of the liver and cirrhosis. The UK Advisory CommiXee on the Safety of Blood, Tissues and Organs (SaBTO) recommends that immunocompromised /immunosuppressed paTents should receive HEV negaTve blood components (10) . All blood components in the UK are HEV tested. However, neither irradiated components nor CMV negaTve components (unless the paTent is a neonate or pregnant), are required.

texts.orig: Anaemia post-transplant is extremely common; the aeTology of which is mulT-factorial and influenced by the Tming post-transplant (Table 1) (1-3) .
texts.text: Anaemia post-transplant is extremely common; the aeTology of which is mulT-factorial and influenced by the Tming post-transplant (Table 1) (1-3) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Apart from the usual causes of anaemia due to CKD, kidney transplant recipients have various unique factors predisposing to anaemia.
texts.text: Apart from the usual causes of anaemia due to CKD, kidney transplant recipients have various unique factors predisposing to anaemia.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Factors causing post-transplant anaemia (PTA):
texts.text: Factors causing post-transplant anaemia (PTA):

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. GFR: anaemia in people with a kidney transplant reflects the degree of GFR similar to other people with CKD (3) .
texts.text: GFR: anaemia in people with a kidney transplant reflects the degree of GFR similar to other people with CKD (3) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Immunosuppressive medicaTons: Mycophenolate and azathioprine are myelosuppressive agents. Calcineurin inhibitors may cause anaemia by microangiopathic haemolysis (4-8) . OKT3 may also cause haemolyTc uraemic syndrome (HUS) (9-10) . Tacrolimus has also been associated with anaemia (11-13) . It may interfere with post erythropoieTn receptor binding intracellular signalling and may occasionally cause HUS (13-14) .
texts.text: Immunosuppressive medicaTons: Mycophenolate and azathioprine are myelosuppressive agents. Calcineurin inhibitors may cause anaemia by microangiopathic haemolysis (4-8) . OKT3 may also cause haemolyTc uraemic syndrome (HUS) (9-10) . Tacrolimus has also been associated with anaemia (11-13) . It may interfere with post erythropoieTn receptor binding intracellular signalling and may occasionally cause HUS (13-14) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Angiotensin converTng enzyme (ACE) inhibitor and angiotensin receptor blocker (ARB) use: ACE inhibiTon has been linked with anaemia (3, 15) . Its pathogenesis is mulTfactorial and may include inhibiTon of endogenous EPO producTon, producTon of an erythropoiesis-inhibiTng protein (16) and inhibiTon of angiotensin II mediated sTmulaTon of erythrocyte precursors (17)
texts.text: Angiotensin converTng enzyme (ACE) inhibitor and angiotensin receptor blocker (ARB) use: ACE inhibiTon has been linked with anaemia (3, 15) . Its pathogenesis is mulTfactorial and may include inhibiTon of endogenous EPO producTon, producTon of an erythropoiesis-inhibiTng protein (16) and inhibiTon of angiotensin II mediated sTmulaTon of erythrocyte precursors (17)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. AnTbioTc use: various common anTbioTcs may cause anaemia including trimethoprimsulfamethoxazole.
texts.text: AnTbioTc use: various common anTbioTcs may cause anaemia including trimethoprimsulfamethoxazole.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. InfecTons: viral infecTons such as cytomegalovirus and parvovirus B19 and anTviral agents such as ganciclovir may cause anaemia in people with a kidney transplant (18-19) .
texts.text: InfecTons: viral infecTons such as cytomegalovirus and parvovirus B19 and anTviral agents such as ganciclovir may cause anaemia in people with a kidney transplant (18-19) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6. Malignancy: malignancies including post-transplant lymphoproliferaTve disorder may result in anaemia.
texts.text: Malignancy: malignancies including post-transplant lymphoproliferaTve disorder may result in anaemia.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 7. HaemolyTc anaemia: haemolyTc anaemia may result from HUS or minor blood group incompaTbility in people with a kidney transplant (20-22) .
texts.text: HaemolyTc anaemia: haemolyTc anaemia may result from HUS or minor blood group incompaTbility in people with a kidney transplant (20-22) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 8. RejecTon episodes: Acute rejecTon may cause reduced endogenous EPO producTon (23) . Severe vascular rejecTon may cause microangiopathy.
texts.text: RejecTon episodes: Acute rejecTon may cause reduced endogenous EPO producTon (23) . Severe vascular rejecTon may cause microangiopathy.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 9. Chronic inflammaTon: Failing kidney transplant causes a chronic inflammatory state resulTng in EPO hypo-responsiveness.
texts.text: Chronic inflammaTon: Failing kidney transplant causes a chronic inflammatory state resulTng in EPO hypo-responsiveness.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Early post-transplant anaemia (PTA) can be seen in the first 6 months following transplantaTon due to iron deficiency and the slow producTon of EPO from the new transplant graV (24) .
texts.text: Early post-transplant anaemia (PTA) can be seen in the first 6 months following transplantaTon due to iron deficiency and the slow producTon of EPO from the new transplant graV (24) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although blood loss occurring via the transplant surgery itself will contribute to early post-transplant anaemia, transfusion usually occurs in the first two weeks post-transplant (25) .  Endogenous EPO is sustained from 28 days post-transplant in the seLng of primary graV funcTon (29) .  Studies have shown that in the early post-transplant period ESA (erythropoiesis sTmulaTng agent) is safe and effecTve, although the dose required may be higher than in the pre-transplant period (30, 31) .  Given the increasing use of marginal donor kidneys, associated with delayed graV funcTon and subopTmal funcTon, there may be a longer requirement for EPO.

Given the increasing use of marginal donor kidneys, associated with delayed graV funcTon and subopTmal funcTon, there may be a longer requirement for EPO.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When a red cell transfusion is required clinically post-transplant in the stable paTent, single unit transfusions should be uTlised where possible (3) .  Although red cell transfusions are recognised to be associated with HLA sensiTsaTon in the pre-transplant seLng, less data are available on the risk of de novo alloimmune responses post-transplant whilst receiving immunosuppression (25, 32) . There are data that the development of de novo transfusion specific anTbodies post-transplant can occur posttransplant; plus, addiTonal evidence of a potenTal link between the receipt of a red cell transfusion and de novo donor specific anTbodies (32) . LimiTng exposure to mulTple red cell donors is therefore advisable, if possible.

texts.orig: Late transplant anaemia occurs more than 6 months aVer transplantaTon and can be seen in up to 36% of paTents (33) . This is influenced by the funcTon of the transplant graV, but also a combinaTon of the factors listed above as well as iron deficiency.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: AVer the early post-transplant period, the frequency of transfusions is markedly reduced. Anaemia management is more in line with that of CKD, however, there remain specific risk factors for anaemia which will be present and need consideraTon in terms of reversibility and management.
texts.text: AVer the early post-transplant period, the frequency of transfusions is markedly reduced. Anaemia management is more in line with that of CKD, however, there remain specific risk factors for anaemia which will be present and need consideraTon in terms of reversibility and management.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The JACK-II study looked at the associaTon between PTA and graV funcTon in 1307 kidney transplant recipients. This study found that 46.3% of the cohort had PTA at 7 years post transplantaTon, with low Hb levels associated to an increase in transplant graV failure (hazard raTo = 1.83, 95% CI: 1.662.02, P < 0.001) (34) .
texts.text: The JACK-II study looked at the associaTon between PTA and graV funcTon in 1307 kidney transplant recipients. This study found that 46.3% of the cohort had PTA at 7 years post transplantaTon, with low Hb levels associated to an increase in transplant graV failure (hazard raTo = 1.83, 95% CI: 1.662.02, P < 0.001) (34) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This associaTon was previously reported in the TransQol-HU Study, looking at a cohort of 938 kidney transplant recipients with a 4-year follow-up period. This study showed that mortality and graV failure were significantly higher in people who were anaemic vs those who were not anaemic. Mortality rates were reported at 18% vs 10% (p<0.001) and graV failure rates were 17% vs 6% (p<0.001) (35) .
texts.text: This associaTon was previously reported in the TransQol-HU Study, looking at a cohort of 938 kidney transplant recipients with a 4-year follow-up period. This study showed that mortality and graV failure were significantly higher in people who were anaemic vs those who were not anaemic. Mortality rates were reported at 18% vs 10% (p<0.001) and graV failure rates were 17% vs 6% (p<0.001) (35) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: GaVer-Gvili et al. assessed 266 people and found that 51.3% experienced early PTA and 36.6% experiences late PTA. The study found that there was higher mortality aVer 4 years in those who experienced early PTA, which processed to late PTA vs those who did not have anaemia and lower Hb was associated with increased mortality (hazard raTo [HR] 0.716, 95% confidence intervals [CI] 0.541-0.948, for every increment of 1 g/dL) and increase graV failure (HR 0.775, 95% CI: 0.619-0.969, for every increment of 1 g/dL) (36) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Scechter et al. (33) also found similar results in their cohort of 1139 kidney transplant recipients. 36.2% of the cohort had PTA and 11.7% had severe anaemia (Hb <11g/dL). Early PTA was associated with graV loss or mortality (hazard raTo (HR) 3.64, 95% CI: 2.34-5.66, p < 0.001) but a weaker associaTon as seen with late PTA (HR 1.44, 95% CI: 0.97-2.13, 0.07). Severe anaemia was associated with an increase in all-cause mortality. It was also found that PTA secondary to AKI, acute rejecTon, infecTon is associated with a higher risk of death or graV loss (HR 9.32, 95% CI: 5.3-26.41, p < 0.001 and HR 3.99, 95% CI: 2.01-7.95, p < 0.001, respecTvely) (33) .

Early PTA was associated with graV loss or mortality (hazard raTo (HR) 3.64, 95% CI: 2.34-5.66, p < 0.001) but a weaker associaTon as seen with late PTA (HR 1.44, 95% CI: 0.97-2.13, 0.07). Severe anaemia was associated with an increase in all-cause mortality. It was also found that PTA secondary to AKI, acute rejecTon, infecTon is associated with a higher risk of death or graV loss (HR 9.32, 95% CI: 5.3-26.41, p < 0.001 and HR 3.99, 95% CI: 2.01-7.95, p < 0.001, respecTvely) (33) .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Safety of ESA in transplant pa9ents
texts.text: Safety of ESA in transplant pa9ents

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A few early retrospecTve studies suggested increased incidence of delayed graV funcTon in people on ESA prior to transplantaTon (37, 38) . However, Registry data has since shown reduced incidence of delayed graV funcTon despite increasing use of ESA. It has also been shown that ESA use prior to kidney transplantaTon does not reduce producTon of or response to endogenous EPO (39, 40) . Studies in the early post-transplant period did not show significant adverse events including delayed graV funcTon or hypertension (41, 42) . Studies in the late transplant period have shown increased incidence of hypertension (43, 44) . ESAs, most probably, do not accelerate rate of graV funcTon decline and one study suggested that correcTon of anaemia slowed the decline in allograV funcTon (45) .
texts.text: A few early retrospecTve studies suggested increased incidence of delayed graV funcTon in people on ESA prior to transplantaTon (37, 38) . However, Registry data has since shown reduced incidence of delayed graV funcTon despite increasing use of ESA. It has also been shown that ESA use prior to kidney transplantaTon does not reduce producTon of or response to endogenous EPO (39, 40) . Studies in the early post-transplant period did not show significant adverse events including delayed graV funcTon or hypertension (41, 42) . Studies in the late transplant period have shown increased incidence of hypertension (43, 44) . ESAs, most probably, do not accelerate rate of graV funcTon decline and one study suggested that correcTon of anaemia slowed the decline in allograV funcTon (45) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In another prospecTve study that assessed the effect of correcTon of anaemia on progression of kidney Insufficiency in people with a kidney transplant; 128 people from 17 centres in France treated with ESA were randomised to full correcTon of anaemia (haemoglobin values130-150 g/l, n =63) versus parTal correcTon of anaemia (Hb value 105-115 g/l, n =62).This study found that in the group of paTents with a haemoglobin level close to normal (~130 g/L), the rate of decline of kidney funcTon was lower compared with the group of control parTcipants, and the number of people reaching end-stage kidney disease and the number of graV failures was lower in this treatment group compared with the control group, suggesTng that correcTng anaemia in people with a kidney transplant reduces the rate of decrease of kidney funcTon and reduces the number of graVs lost (46) .
texts.text: In another prospecTve study that assessed the effect of correcTon of anaemia on progression of kidney Insufficiency in people with a kidney transplant; 128 people from 17 centres in France treated with ESA were randomised to full correcTon of anaemia (haemoglobin values130-150 g/l, n =63) versus parTal correcTon of anaemia (Hb value 105-115 g/l, n =62).This study found that in the group of paTents with a haemoglobin level close to normal (~130 g/L), the rate of decline of kidney funcTon was lower compared with the group of control parTcipants, and the number of people reaching end-stage kidney disease and the number of graV failures was lower in this treatment group compared with the control group, suggesTng that correcTng anaemia in people with a kidney transplant reduces the rate of decrease of kidney funcTon and reduces the number of graVs lost (46) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A similar study from Japan compared the effect of maintaining higher Hb levels (125-135 g/L, n=64) using ESAs with maintaining Hb at 105-115 g/L (n=63). This study found that those people in the lower Hb target group (mean levels 115 ± 12 g/L) had significantly higher rate of eGFR decline at 12, 18, 24 and 36 (p=0.02) months, compared to those in the higher Hb target group. Similar levels of adverse events were seen in both groups (47) .
texts.text: A similar study from Japan compared the effect of maintaining higher Hb levels (125-135 g/L, n=64) using ESAs with maintaining Hb at 105-115 g/L (n=63). This study found that those people in the lower Hb target group (mean levels 115 ± 12 g/L) had significantly higher rate of eGFR decline at 12, 18, 24 and 36 (p=0.02) months, compared to those in the higher Hb target group. Similar levels of adverse events were seen in both groups (47) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A further single centre study showed no change in the rate of decline in allograV funcTon over 2 years with epoeTn beta therapy (target Hb 115-135 g/L). However, the study did show significant increase in the vitality and mental health domains of the medical outcomes short form health survey (48) .
texts.text: A further single centre study showed no change in the rate of decline in allograV funcTon over 2 years with epoeTn beta therapy (target Hb 115-135 g/L). However, the study did show significant increase in the vitality and mental health domains of the medical outcomes short form health survey (48) .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Efficacy of ESA in people with a kidney transplant
texts.text: Efficacy of ESA in people with a kidney transplant

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Studies in the early post-transplant period have shown that ESA is effecTve in these people, although the dose required may be higher than in pre-transplant period (41, 42) . Similarly, studies in late posttransplant period have shown efficacy of ESA in these people (43, 44, 49, 50) .
texts.text: Studies in the early post-transplant period have shown that ESA is effecTve in these people, although the dose required may be higher than in pre-transplant period (41, 42) . Similarly, studies in late posttransplant period have shown efficacy of ESA in these people (43, 44, 49, 50) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that the treatment guidelines for anaemia in kidney transplant recipients should be similar to those for people with CKD not receiving dialysis (NDD-CKD). (1B)
texts.text: We recommend that the treatment guidelines for anaemia in kidney transplant recipients should be similar to those for people with CKD not receiving dialysis (NDD-CKD). (1B)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest for stable non-bleeding individuals who clinically require a red cell transfusion, using single unit transfusion, where possible. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 6.3.3
texts.text: Guideline 6.3.3

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest the use of HIF-PHI therapy in transplant recipients should be as in people with NDD-CKD. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest the use of HIF-PHI therapy in transplant recipients should be as in people with NDD-CKD. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Rationale
texts.text: Rationale

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Data on the use of HIF-PHI therapy in kidney transplant recipients are limited to small case series and non-randomised real-world evidence.  In the randomised controlled trials transplant paTents were excluded in the majority and hence data is limited to draw any conclusions.  However, in resports thus far, the use of HIF-PHI therapy appears to be effecTve in raising Hb levels to the current recommended target range in the short term with limited documented adverse effects (1-2) . The numbers of people treated are too small to currently make any specific recommendaTons of use of HIF-PHI in people with a kidney transaplant (1-2) . In the recent published studies from Asia, there were approximately 73 paTents in the 4 studies from Asia (3-6) . A growing experience exists in Europe and the UK (personal communicaTon). A specific consideraTon in the transplant populaTon, would be the addiTve theoreTcal risk of malignancy in the long term.
texts.text: Data on the use of HIF-PHI therapy in kidney transplant recipients are limited to small case series and non-randomised real-world evidence.  In the randomised controlled trials transplant paTents were excluded in the majority and hence data is limited to draw any conclusions. texts.text: Guideline 7.1 - Malignancy:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We suggest treaTng symptomaTc anaemia in people with CKD and cancer in those with a Hb <100 g/L. (2B)
texts.text: We suggest treaTng symptomaTc anaemia in people with CKD and cancer in those with a Hb <100 g/L. (2B)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Rationale
texts.text: Rationale

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Iron replacement may be used to improve haemoglobin response, and reduce RBC transfusions, for  paTents  receiving  ESA  with  or  without  iron  deficiency.  If  paTents  are  receiving  cardiotoxic chemotherapy, iron must NOT be administered on the same day.
texts.text: Iron replacement may be used to improve haemoglobin response, and reduce RBC transfusions, for  paTents  receiving  ESA  with  or  without  iron  deficiency.  If  paTents  are  receiving  cardiotoxic chemotherapy, iron must NOT be administered on the same day.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · ESAs may be offered to paTents, on acTve treatment for cancer (chemotherapy), whose cancer treatment is not curaTve.
texts.text: ESAs may be offered to paTents, on acTve treatment for cancer (chemotherapy), whose cancer treatment is not curaTve.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · If a person has an acTve cancer, NOT having chemotherapy treatment, DO NOT give ESA's.
texts.text: If a person has an acTve cancer, NOT having chemotherapy treatment, DO NOT give ESA's.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · If a person had a previous cancer which is deemed cured, then there are no concerns for giving ESA's.
texts.text: If a person had a previous cancer which is deemed cured, then there are no concerns for giving ESA's.

texts.text: We suggest ESA therapy be considered in people with myelodysplasTc syndrome (MDS), myeloma and others with CKD and haematological disease. We make no recommendaTon on HIF-PHI therapy. (not graded)

texts.orig: ESA should be considered in people with MDS with low risk MDS and symptomaTc anaemia and Hb <100g/L.  If  a  person  with  MDS  does  not  respond  to  ESA  therapy  within  8-12  weeks  then Haematologists should consider commencing G-CSF.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 7.3 - Pregnancy:
texts.text: Guideline 7.3 - Pregnancy:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that pregnant and post-partum women with CKD are given parenteral iron, if indicated, from the second trimester onwards and if the benefit is judged to outweigh the potenTal risk for both the mother and the foetus according to SmPC guidance. (1C)
texts.text: We recommend that pregnant and post-partum women with CKD are given parenteral iron, if indicated, from the second trimester onwards and if the benefit is judged to outweigh the potenTal risk for both the mother and the foetus according to SmPC guidance. (1C)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 7.3.1
texts.text: Guideline 7.3.1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that ESA therapy during pregnancy may be conTnued unless there is a major contraindicaTon (e.g., hypertension or thrombosis risks). (2C)
texts.text: We recommend that ESA therapy during pregnancy may be conTnued unless there is a major contraindicaTon (e.g., hypertension or thrombosis risks). (2C)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Rationale
texts.text: Rationale

texts.label: text
texts.orig: During pregnancy, plasma volumes become higher than the increase in red blood cell mass, which leads to lower observed haemoglobin levels. The lower reference limit for haemoglobin concentraTons in pregnancy is 110 g/L in the first trimester, 105 g/L in the second trimester and 100 g/L in the post-partum period (1, 2), with values <85 g/L associated with an esTmated 62% increase in the risk of low birth weight (<2,500 g) and a 72% increase in the risk of preterm delivery before 37 weeks, across ethnic groups (3) . There are no data to guide the opTmum target haemoglobin for women with CKD in pregnancy (4) .
texts.text: During pregnancy, plasma volumes become higher than the increase in red blood cell mass, which leads to lower observed haemoglobin levels. The lower reference limit for haemoglobin concentraTons in pregnancy is 110 g/L in the first trimester, 105 g/L in the second trimester and 100 g/L in the post-partum period (1, 2), with values <85 g/L associated with an esTmated 62% increase in the risk of low birth weight (<2,500 g) and a 72% increase in the risk of preterm delivery before 37 weeks, across ethnic groups (3) . There are no data to guide the opTmum target haemoglobin for women with CKD in pregnancy (4) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The most common cause of anaemia in pregnancy is iron deficiency, which is esTmated to affect greater than 40% of pregnancies (5) . Iron deficiency in pregnancy can contribute to maternal mortality, increased maternal morbidity, impaired infant development and potenTal negaTve effects on pregnancy outcomes (low birth weight, placental abrupTon, peripartum blood loss) (2) .
texts.text: The most common cause of anaemia in pregnancy is iron deficiency, which is esTmated to affect greater than 40% of pregnancies (5) . Iron deficiency in pregnancy can contribute to maternal mortality, increased maternal morbidity, impaired infant development and potenTal negaTve effects on pregnancy outcomes (low birth weight, placental abrupTon, peripartum blood loss) (2) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pregnant women are normally offered oral iron in the first instance if Hb is below target value, however IV iron is offered if they cannot tolerate oral iron or the Hb has not responded to oral therapy (2, 6) . Parenteral iron is considered safe in pregnancy and breasGLYPH<145>eeding (7-10) , although there is a paucity of safety data on exposure in the first trimester. It is important to review the SmPC for each pregnant person as foetal bradycardia may occur following administraTon of parenteral irons. It is usually transient and a consequence of a hypersensiTvity reacTon in the mother. The unborn baby should be carefully monitored during intravenous administraTon of parenteral irons to pregnant women.
texts.text: Pregnant women are normally offered oral iron in the first instance if Hb is below target value, however IV iron is offered if they cannot tolerate oral iron or the Hb has not responded to oral therapy (2, 6) . Parenteral iron is considered safe in pregnancy and breasGLYPH<145>eeding (7-10) , although there is a paucity of safety data on exposure in the first trimester. It is important to review the SmPC for each pregnant person as foetal bradycardia may occur following administraTon of parenteral irons. It is usually transient and a consequence of a hypersensiTvity reacTon in the mother. The unborn baby should be carefully monitored during intravenous administraTon of parenteral irons to pregnant women.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recent reviews have commented that ESA therapy are considered safe in pregnancy, as they are large molecules which is unlikely to cross the placenta (11, 12) . They are also considered safe during lactaTon. OVen twice to three Tmes the use dose is required during pregnancy to achieve the desired haemoglobin target.
texts.text: Recent reviews have commented that ESA therapy are considered safe in pregnancy, as they are large molecules which is unlikely to cross the placenta (11, 12) . They are also considered safe during lactaTon. OVen twice to three Tmes the use dose is required during pregnancy to achieve the desired haemoglobin target.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These recommendaTons are consistent with Guideline 4.6.1 of the UKKA pregnancy in CKD guidelines which states that 'We recommend pregnant women with CKD are given parenteral iron if indicated'.
texts.text: These recommendaTons are consistent with Guideline 4.6.1 of the UKKA pregnancy in CKD guidelines which states that 'We recommend pregnant women with CKD are given parenteral iron if indicated'.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: New Therapies for Consideration in the future
texts.text: New Therapies for Consideration in the future

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several new areas of drug development are ongoing including inhibitors of hepcidin producTon or acTon, anT-interleukin-6 specific anTbodies (ZilTvekimab), anT-inflammatory biologicals, and acTvin receptor ligand traps (1) .
texts.text: Several new areas of drug development are ongoing including inhibitors of hepcidin producTon or acTon, anT-interleukin-6 specific anTbodies (ZilTvekimab), anT-inflammatory biologicals, and acTvin receptor ligand traps (1) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A phase 1/2, placebo-controlled trial examined the effects of intravenous zilTvekimab every 2 weeks for 12 weeks vs placebo, in 61 people on haemodialy sis with a single nucleoTde polymorphism of the TMPRSS6 gene (putaTvely increases suscepTbility to IL-6-mediated inflammatory effects). ZilTvekimab led to a significantly greater reducTon of high-sensiTvity C-reacTve protein and ESA usage and ESA resistance index. There was also an increased serum iron, total iron binding capacity, transferrin saturaTon, and serum albumin (2) . Future trials may define its benefit.
texts.text: A phase 1/2, placebo-controlled trial examined the effects of intravenous zilTvekimab every 2 weeks for 12 weeks vs placebo, in 61 people on haemodialy sis with a single nucleoTde polymorphism of the TMPRSS6 gene (putaTvely increases suscepTbility to IL-6-mediated inflammatory effects). ZilTvekimab led to a significantly greater reducTon of high-sensiTvity C-reacTve protein and ESA usage and ESA resistance index. There was also an increased serum iron, total iron binding capacity, transferrin saturaTon, and serum albumin (2) . Future trials may define its benefit.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PRS080#022, a PEGylated (polyethylene glycol bound) anTcalin protein, antagonizes hepcidin with picomolar affinity (3) . Two first-in-human randomised, double-blind phase 1 trials of PRS-080#22; a single intravenous infusion of placebo or PRS-080#22 was administered to 48 healthy volunteers (phase 1a) and 24 paTents on hemodialysis (phase 1b) at different doses (0.08-16 mg/kg for the phase 1a study and 2-8mg/kg for the phase 1b study) in successive dosing cohorts. This led to a dose dependent reducTon in serum hepcidin levels, mobilisaTon of serum iron with increased serum iron concentraTon and transferrin saturaTon. There was impact on ferriTn or haemoglobin. This drug was safe and effecTve but further clinical studies are needed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Neutralising hepcidin with a monoclonal anTbody (mAb) may prevent ferroporTn internalizaTon, restore iron efflux from cells, and allow transferrin-mediated iron transport to the bone marrow. A mulT-centre, phase 1 study evaluated the safety, pharmacokineTcs (PK), pharmacodynamics (PD), and efficacy of a fully humanized mAb (LY2787106) with high affinity for hepcidin in cancer paTents with anaemia (4) . Thirty-three paTents with hepcidin levels ≥5 ng/mL received LY2787106 either every 3 weeks (19 paTents, dose range 0.3-10 mg/kg) (part A) or weekly (14 paTents, dose 10 mg/kg) (part B). Consistent dose-dependent increases in serum iron, and transferrin saturaTon were seen at the 3 and 10 mg/kg dose levels, typically peaking within 24 h aVer LY2787106 administraTon and returning to baseline by day 8. LY2787106 resulted in transient iron mobilizaTon, thus supporTng the role of hepcidin in iron regulaTon (4) .

texts.orig: Currently animal data is available suggesTng that BMP6 is a key endogenous regulator of hepcidin (7, 8) . DeleTons in genes encoding the ligand BMP6, the BMP coreceptor HJV, the BMP Type I receptors ALK2 and ALK3, or the intracellular signalling molecule SMAD4 (9-11) , all independently resulted in inappropriately suppressed hepcidin expression and Tssue iron overload. Recent studies examining te use of anT-haemojuvelin anTbodies have demonstrated improvements in anaemia (12) . texts.orig: Aptamers are syntheTc single-stranded oligonucleoTdes that bind ligands with high affinity, represenTng a novel class of oligonucleoTde structures suitable for blocking purposes (14) . Spiegelmers are mirror image aptamers. Spiegelmers are aXracTve therapeuTc agents and NOX-H94 is a polyethylene glycol conjugated (PEGylated) spiegelmer that targets and binds to human hepcidin (15) . The first human trial is currently underway (15, 16) .

An anTferroporTn mAb which binds to the extracellular loop of ferroporTn, can block the hepcidin-ferroporTn interacTon, while maintaining ferroporTn funcTon (17) . This strategy would promote ferroporTn acTvity and allow conTnuous iron influx, potenTally prevenTng iron-restricTve erythropoiesis due to hepcidin excess. Human studies are awaited.

texts.orig: SGLT2 inhibitors have been shown to increase Hb in people with chronic kidney disease and/ or heart failure (18-23) . The increased Hb in people treated with SGLT2 inhibitors seems to be independent of diureTc use or as a result of intravascular volume depleTon causing haemoconcentraTon (24, 25) . Data suggest that SGLT2 inhibitor administraTon is associated with transient increases in serum EPO concentraTons (30%-40%), an increase in reTculocyte counts or haematocrit levels (26) and a decrease in ferriTn and hepcidin, indicaTng erythropoieTc sTmulaTon (2730) . However, more informaTon is needed to beXer understand the mechanisms of acTon underlying these effects and their clinical relevance.

texts.text: Lay Summary

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Anaemia is a commonly diagnosed complicaTon among people suffering with chronic kidney disease. If leV untreated, it may affect quality of life. There are several causes for anaemia in this populaTon. As the kidney funcTon deteriorates, together with medicaTons and dietary restricTons, people with chronic kidney disease may develop iron deficiency, resulTng in reducTon of iron supply to the bone marrow (which is the body organ responsible for the producTon of different blood elements). People with chronic kidney disease may not be able to use their own body's iron stores well and hence, many people, parTcularly those receiving haemodialysis, may need addiTonal iron treatment, usually provided by injecTon.
texts.text: Anaemia is a commonly diagnosed complicaTon among people suffering with chronic kidney disease. If leV untreated, it may affect quality of life. There are several causes for anaemia in this populaTon. As the kidney funcTon deteriorates, together with medicaTons and dietary restricTons, people with chronic kidney disease may develop iron deficiency, resulTng in reducTon of iron supply to the bone marrow (which is the body organ responsible for the producTon of different blood elements). People with chronic kidney disease may not be able to use their own body's iron stores well and hence, many people, parTcularly those receiving haemodialysis, may need addiTonal iron treatment, usually provided by injecTon.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With further weakening of kidney funcTon, people with chronic kidney disease may need addiTonal treatment with a substance called erythropoieTn which drives the bone marrow to produce its own blood. This substance, which is naturally produced by the kidneys, becomes relaTvely deficient in people with chronic kidney disease. These people will eventually need treatment with erythropoieTn or similar products that are given by injecTon.
texts.text: With further weakening of kidney funcTon, people with chronic kidney disease may need addiTonal treatment with a substance called erythropoieTn which drives the bone marrow to produce its own blood. This substance, which is naturally produced by the kidneys, becomes relaTvely deficient in people with chronic kidney disease. These people will eventually need treatment with erythropoieTn or similar products that are given by injecTon.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Over the last few years, several iron and erythropoieTn products (syntheTc products and those sTmulaTng the body's own erythropoieTn) have been licensed for treaTng anaemia in people with chronic kidney disease. In addiTon, several publicaTons discussed the benefits of each treatment and possible risks associated with long term treatment. The current guidelines provide advice to health care professionals on how to screen people with chronic kidney disease for anaemia, which people to invesTgate for other causes of anaemia, when and how to treat people with different medicaTons, how to ensure safe prescribing and monitoring of treatment and how to diagnose and manage complicaTons associated with anaemia and the drugs used for its treatment.
texts.text: Over the last few years, several iron and erythropoieTn products (syntheTc products and those sTmulaTng the body's own erythropoieTn) have been licensed for treaTng anaemia in people with chronic kidney disease. In addiTon, several publicaTons discussed the benefits of each treatment and possible risks associated with long term treatment. The current guidelines provide advice to health care professionals on how to screen people with chronic kidney disease for anaemia, which people to invesTgate for other causes of anaemia, when and how to treat people with different medicaTons, how to ensure safe prescribing and monitoring of treatment and how to diagnose and manage complicaTons associated with anaemia and the drugs used for its treatment.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Pa9ent Informa9on Sheet:
texts.text: Pa9ent Informa9on Sheet:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the kidney's main jobs is to help make red blood cells. The kidneys make a hormone called erythropoieTn that is a signal for the body to make red blood cells.
texts.text: One of the kidney's main jobs is to help make red blood cells. The kidneys make a hormone called erythropoieTn that is a signal for the body to make red blood cells.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If your kidneys don't work as well as they should, they make less erythropoieTn, so fewer red blood cells are made. Iron levels in the body are usually low in people with advanced kidney disease.
texts.text: If your kidneys don't work as well as they should, they make less erythropoieTn, so fewer red blood cells are made. Iron levels in the body are usually low in people with advanced kidney disease.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The medical term for this condiTon is Anaemia of Chronic Kidney Disease. It means a low amount of red blood cells in the body caused by long-term kidney disease.
texts.text: The medical term for this condiTon is Anaemia of Chronic Kidney Disease. It means a low amount of red blood cells in the body caused by long-term kidney disease.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: People with anaemia oVen feel Tred and short of breath, but there can be other symptoms.
texts.text: People with anaemia oVen feel Tred and short of breath, but there can be other symptoms.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We treat anaemia with medicines including iron tablets and iron injecTons. We also use ESA (also called EPO) injecTons and other medicaTon (HIF-PHI) that help make erythropoieTn and help make iron work beXer in the body.
texts.text: We treat anaemia with medicines including iron tablets and iron injecTons. We also use ESA (also called EPO) injecTons and other medicaTon (HIF-PHI) that help make erythropoieTn and help make iron work beXer in the body.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 1 - Evalua9on of anaemia - Baseline inves9ga9ons
texts.text: Guideline 1 - Evalua9on of anaemia - Baseline inves9ga9ons

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Subjects with chronic kidney (CKD) disease who develop anaemia need iniTal invesTgaTons in the form of blood tests to:
texts.text: Subjects with chronic kidney (CKD) disease who develop anaemia need iniTal invesTgaTons in the form of blood tests to:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Assess the severity of anaemia
texts.text: Assess the severity of anaemia

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Assess the cause of anaemia: some subjects with CKD could develop anaemia due to causes other than kidney disease: such as bleeding, iron & vitamin deficiency or many other causes. It is important to perform all the required tests before concluding that the anaemia is due to CKD.
texts.text: Assess the cause of anaemia: some subjects with CKD could develop anaemia due to causes other than kidney disease: such as bleeding, iron & vitamin deficiency or many other causes. It is important to perform all the required tests before concluding that the anaemia is due to CKD.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.1 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron therapy:
texts.text: Guideline 2.1 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron therapy:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Iron is one of the building blocks of red cell formaTon by the body. Therefore correcTon of iron deficiency is an important iniTal step in the treatment of anaemia of CKD.
texts.text: Iron is one of the building blocks of red cell formaTon by the body. Therefore correcTon of iron deficiency is an important iniTal step in the treatment of anaemia of CKD.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.2 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron Therapy - Ini9a9on of ESA/HIF-PHI and Iron Status:
texts.text: Guideline 2.2 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron Therapy - Ini9a9on of ESA/HIF-PHI and Iron Status:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · There are several specific blood tests arranged regularly through the kidney clinic to ensure the adequacy of iron stores in the body of subjects with CKD, and to assess how easily these iron stores are uTlized by the body to correct anaemia.
texts.text: There are several specific blood tests arranged regularly through the kidney clinic to ensure the adequacy of iron stores in the body of subjects with CKD, and to assess how easily these iron stores are uTlized by the body to correct anaemia.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Many subjects could have their anaemia corrected by receiving iron therapy only.
texts.text: Many subjects could have their anaemia corrected by receiving iron therapy only.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Therefore, it is important to ensure that people with CKD have adequate iron stores prior to considering the iniTaTon of other drugs like ESA or HIF-PHI.
texts.text: Therefore, it is important to ensure that people with CKD have adequate iron stores prior to considering the iniTaTon of other drugs like ESA or HIF-PHI.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.3 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron therapy - Route of Administra9on
texts.text: Guideline 2.3 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron therapy - Route of Administra9on

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Oral iron tablets are effecTve in correcTng iron deficiency in the majority of CKD subjects with anaemia.
texts.text: Oral iron tablets are effecTve in correcTng iron deficiency in the majority of CKD subjects with anaemia.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Intravenous iron:
texts.text: Intravenous iron:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Intravenous iron (Iron injecTon given by a drip into the vein, IV iron), is usually needed when iron tablets are not effecTve or not tolerated by subjects with CKD.
texts.text: Intravenous iron (Iron injecTon given by a drip into the vein, IV iron), is usually needed when iron tablets are not effecTve or not tolerated by subjects with CKD.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · IV iron is widely used to correct iron deficiency among people receiving haemodialysis.
texts.text: IV iron is widely used to correct iron deficiency among people receiving haemodialysis.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · IV iron is also given rouTnely to people before starTng on ESA of HIF-PHI therapy to make this treatment more effecTve.
texts.text: IV iron is also given rouTnely to people before starTng on ESA of HIF-PHI therapy to make this treatment more effecTve.

texts.orig: UK Kidney Associa.on Clinical Prac.ce Guidelines for Anaemia of Chronic Kidney Disease                                                                             111
texts.text: UK Kidney Associa.on Clinical Prac.ce Guidelines for Anaemia of Chronic Kidney Disease                                                                             111

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · There are different preparaTons of IV iron. Some preparaTons are given in a small dose at frequent intervals, others are given as large dose infused over few hours.
texts.text: There are different preparaTons of IV iron. Some preparaTons are given in a small dose at frequent intervals, others are given as large dose infused over few hours.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · People receiving large dose iron infusions will not require frequent treatment unlike those receiving small dose preparaTons.
texts.text: People receiving large dose iron infusions will not require frequent treatment unlike those receiving small dose preparaTons.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Small dose, more frequent IV iron is suitable for people receiving In Centre Haemodialysis, while large dose, less frequent preparaTons are suitable for people with CKD not receiving dialysis or those receiving peritoneal dialysis.
texts.text: Small dose, more frequent IV iron is suitable for people receiving In Centre Haemodialysis, while large dose, less frequent preparaTons are suitable for people with CKD not receiving dialysis or those receiving peritoneal dialysis.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 2.4 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron therapy - safety and Upper limit for iron therapy.
texts.text: Guideline 2.4 - Treatment of Anaemia with Iron therapy - safety and Upper limit for iron therapy.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Iron treatment, parTcularly IV iron, is not without risks, they are administered guided by the value of iron level in the blood.
texts.text: Iron treatment, parTcularly IV iron, is not without risks, they are administered guided by the value of iron level in the blood.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · There are several tests available to assess iron level in the blood and body.
texts.text: There are several tests available to assess iron level in the blood and body.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The commonest test is 'serum FerriTn'.
texts.text: The commonest test is 'serum FerriTn'.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Usually the aim of treatment with iron is to achieve serum ferriTn level between 500-700 mcg/L.
texts.text: Usually the aim of treatment with iron is to achieve serum ferriTn level between 500-700 mcg/L.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · IV iron treatment should be withheld in the presence of any acTve infecTon. It should also be avoided in people with acTve HepaTTs C virus infecTon, in some people with chronic liver disease and those with hereditary liver condiTons.
texts.text: IV iron treatment should be withheld in the presence of any acTve infecTon. It should also be avoided in people with acTve HepaTTs C virus infecTon, in some people with chronic liver disease and those with hereditary liver condiTons.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · To ensure safe treatment with IV iron, people will need regular blood tests (every 1-3 months) to monitor iron level and the response to treatment.
texts.text: To ensure safe treatment with IV iron, people will need regular blood tests (every 1-3 months) to monitor iron level and the response to treatment.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 3. Treatment with Erythropoiesis S9mula9ng Agents
texts.text: Guideline 3. Treatment with Erythropoiesis S9mula9ng Agents

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The aim of treatment of CKD with ESA preparaTons is to improve quality of life and reduce/avoid the need for blood transfusion.
texts.text: The aim of treatment of CKD with ESA preparaTons is to improve quality of life and reduce/avoid the need for blood transfusion.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Like intravenous iron, ESA therapy is not without risks. The doctor will discuss the risk benefit raTo with the receipient/their carer before iniTaTng this treatment.
texts.text: Like intravenous iron, ESA therapy is not without risks. The doctor will discuss the risk benefit raTo with the receipient/their carer before iniTaTng this treatment.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · There are different ESA preparaTons; ome are short acTng preparaTons, needing to be administered more than once weekly, other are longer acTng preparaTons given less frequently (weekly or monthly).
texts.text: There are different ESA preparaTons; ome are short acTng preparaTons, needing to be administered more than once weekly, other are longer acTng preparaTons given less frequently (weekly or monthly).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · ESA preparaTons are given by injecTon: either into the vein (IV) or under the skin (subcutaneous, SC). Although SC injecTon allows lower dose of the drug to produce the desired effect, the choice and route of ESA administraTon is determined by several factors, such as the availability of the preparaTon in the local hospital, the availability of access for IV administraTon and choice of the recipient.
texts.text: ESA preparaTons are given by injecTon: either into the vein (IV) or under the skin (subcutaneous, SC). Although SC injecTon allows lower dose of the drug to produce the desired effect, the choice and route of ESA administraTon is determined by several factors, such as the availability of the preparaTon in the local hospital, the availability of access for IV administraTon and choice of the recipient.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · It will be helpful if the recipient/carer discuss the need for the drug (a shared decision), available opTons and risk benefit raTo of treatment with the health care professional prior to starTng therapy.
texts.text: It will be helpful if the recipient/carer discuss the need for the drug (a shared decision), available opTons and risk benefit raTo of treatment with the health care professional prior to starTng therapy.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · OvercorrecTon of anaemia using ESA therapy could be harmful with adverse effects on the heart and blood pressure. Therefore, treatment with ESA aims to achieve and maintain haemoglobin level between 100 and 120 g/L in adults, and slightly lower in children younger than 2 years old.
texts.text: OvercorrecTon of anaemia using ESA therapy could be harmful with adverse effects on the heart and blood pressure. Therefore, treatment with ESA aims to achieve and maintain haemoglobin level between 100 and 120 g/L in adults, and slightly lower in children younger than 2 years old.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · To achieve and maintain this target Hb range, people receiving ESA therapy will need regular blood tesTng to assess the response to treatment and allow adjustment of ESA dose according to Hb level.
texts.text: To achieve and maintain this target Hb range, people receiving ESA therapy will need regular blood tesTng to assess the response to treatment and allow adjustment of ESA dose according to Hb level.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · ESA therapy is not without risks. Therefore, ESA therapy is a shared decision (balancing benefits and risks) and should be used cauTously/ avoided in people with history of stroke, or in people with certain malignancies.
texts.text: ESA therapy is not without risks. Therefore, ESA therapy is a shared decision (balancing benefits and risks) and should be used cauTously/ avoided in people with history of stroke, or in people with certain malignancies.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Blood pressure needs to be monitored regularly in people receiving ESA, and if needed, blood pressure should be controlled with medicaTons according to local guidelines.
texts.text: Blood pressure needs to be monitored regularly in people receiving ESA, and if needed, blood pressure should be controlled with medicaTons according to local guidelines.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Very rarely, people may become immune against ESA therapy, as a result they become severely anaemic and do not respond to any ESA drugs. People who develop this condiTon should stop
texts.text: Very rarely, people may become immune against ESA therapy, as a result they become severely anaemic and do not respond to any ESA drugs. People who develop this condiTon should stop

texts.label: text
texts.orig: receiving ESA therapy. They may require regular blood transfusions unTl they are treated to regain their ability to respond to ESA therapy.
texts.text: receiving ESA therapy. They may require regular blood transfusions unTl they are treated to regain their ability to respond to ESA therapy.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 4 - Treatment of Anaemia - HIF-PHI Agents
texts.text: Guideline 4 - Treatment of Anaemia - HIF-PHI Agents

texts.label: text
texts.orig: HIF-PHI are newly introduced medicaTons that are now licensed to treat anaemia in CKD subjects. They are administered in the form of oral tablets. They act on a system in the body that is acTvated when the level of oxygen in the blood is low. When acTvated, the body produces more red blood cells. This could be partly through the producTon of more erythropoieTn and partly through beXer use of available iron for blood cell producTon.
texts.text: HIF-PHI are newly introduced medicaTons that are now licensed to treat anaemia in CKD subjects. They are administered in the form of oral tablets. They act on a system in the body that is acTvated when the level of oxygen in the blood is low. When acTvated, the body produces more red blood cells. This could be partly through the producTon of more erythropoieTn and partly through beXer use of available iron for blood cell producTon.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: HIF-PHI tablets acTvate this system, regardless of the oxygen level in the blood. As a result, more red blood cells are produced and anaemia is corrected.
texts.text: HIF-PHI tablets acTvate this system, regardless of the oxygen level in the blood. As a result, more red blood cells are produced and anaemia is corrected.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Like ESA, HIF-PHI treatment is dependent on the amount of iron in the body. Therefore, it is important to treat iron deficiency in any person before prescribing HIF-PHI therapy.
texts.text: Like ESA, HIF-PHI treatment is dependent on the amount of iron in the body. Therefore, it is important to treat iron deficiency in any person before prescribing HIF-PHI therapy.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Treatment with HIF-PHI should conTnue if and when the person is unwell for any other reason. During any illness, like surgery or chest infecTon, anaemia in subjects with CKD could get worse, so treatment of anaemia should conTnue during acute illness unless there is a reason to stop the drug.
texts.text: Treatment with HIF-PHI should conTnue if and when the person is unwell for any other reason. During any illness, like surgery or chest infecTon, anaemia in subjects with CKD could get worse, so treatment of anaemia should conTnue during acute illness unless there is a reason to stop the drug.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · OvercorrecTon of anaemia using HIF-PHI therapy could be harmful with adverse effects on the heart and blood pressure. Therefore, treatment with HIF-PHI therapy aims to achieve and maintain haemoglobin level between 100 and 120 g/L in adults.
texts.text: OvercorrecTon of anaemia using HIF-PHI therapy could be harmful with adverse effects on the heart and blood pressure. Therefore, treatment with HIF-PHI therapy aims to achieve and maintain haemoglobin level between 100 and 120 g/L in adults.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · To achieve and maintain this target Hb range, people receiving HIF-PHI therapy will need regular blood tesTng to assess the response to treatment and allow adjustment of HIF-PHI dose according to Haemoglobin level.
texts.text: To achieve and maintain this target Hb range, people receiving HIF-PHI therapy will need regular blood tesTng to assess the response to treatment and allow adjustment of HIF-PHI dose according to Haemoglobin level.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · HIF-PHII therapy is not without risks. Therefore, this therapy should be used cauTously/ avoided in people with history of stroke or in people with certain malignancies.
texts.text: HIF-PHII therapy is not without risks. Therefore, this therapy should be used cauTously/ avoided in people with history of stroke or in people with certain malignancies.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Blood pressure needs to be monitored regularly in people receiving HIF-PHI, and if needed, blood pressure should be controlled with medicaTons according to local guidelines.
texts.text: Blood pressure needs to be monitored regularly in people receiving HIF-PHI, and if needed, blood pressure should be controlled with medicaTons according to local guidelines.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · HIF-PHI therapy should be avoided in people with acTve malignancy, those with history of epilepsy, history of blood clots and subjects with autosomal dominant polycysTc kidney disease.
texts.text: HIF-PHI therapy should be avoided in people with acTve malignancy, those with history of epilepsy, history of blood clots and subjects with autosomal dominant polycysTc kidney disease.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 5: Blood Transfusion in people with Anaemia of CKD
texts.text: Guideline 5: Blood Transfusion in people with Anaemia of CKD

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Whenever possible, people on the transplant waiTng list should avoid receiving blood transfusions to avoid/ reduce the risks of rejecTon when they receive their transplant.
texts.text: Whenever possible, people on the transplant waiTng list should avoid receiving blood transfusions to avoid/ reduce the risks of rejecTon when they receive their transplant.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Blood transfusion, if needed, should be decided based on each person's requirements, rather than guided by the blood test results only.
texts.text: Blood transfusion, if needed, should be decided based on each person's requirements, rather than guided by the blood test results only.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · People with suppressed immune system, due to kidney transplant or due to any other medical condiTon, and those on the transplant waiTng list should receive blood that is negaTve for hepaTTs E virus.
texts.text: People with suppressed immune system, due to kidney transplant or due to any other medical condiTon, and those on the transplant waiTng list should receive blood that is negaTve for hepaTTs E virus.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 6.0 Peri-transplant and Post Transplant Anaemia
texts.text: Guideline 6.0 Peri-transplant and Post Transplant Anaemia

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Kidney transplant recipients may develop anaemia due to a variety of causes.
texts.text: Kidney transplant recipients may develop anaemia due to a variety of causes.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · It is important to invesTgate and idenTfy the cause of anaemia prior to considering treatment with iron, ESA or HIF-PHI.
texts.text: It is important to invesTgate and idenTfy the cause of anaemia prior to considering treatment with iron, ESA or HIF-PHI.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · If indicated, transplant recipients can be treated with iron, ESA or HIF-PHI as per the guidelines for people with CKD not on dialysis.
texts.text: If indicated, transplant recipients can be treated with iron, ESA or HIF-PHI as per the guidelines for people with CKD not on dialysis.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline 7.0 Special Popula9ons
texts.text: Guideline 7.0 Special Popula9ons

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · SymptomaTc people with CKD and cancer may be treated with Iron & ESA therapy as per the guidelines for people with CKD not on dialysis.
texts.text: SymptomaTc people with CKD and cancer may be treated with Iron & ESA therapy as per the guidelines for people with CKD not on dialysis.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Pregnant subjects with CKD who develop anaemia may be treated with ESA therapy. They may be treated with IV iron from the second trimester.
texts.text: Pregnant subjects with CKD who develop anaemia may be treated with ESA therapy. They may be treated with IV iron from the second trimester.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Pregnant subjects may conTnue ESA therapy during pregnancy.
texts.text: Pregnant subjects may conTnue ESA therapy during pregnancy.
